attractions.name: Animal Ark Wildlife Sanctuary
attractions.description: Animal Ark Wildlife Sanctuary is a 37-acre nonprofit wildlife sanctuary located near Reno, Nevada, providing permanent homes for injured, non-releasable, and surrendered wildlife. The sanctuary is home to native North American and exotic species including cougars, wolves, bears, tigers, and more. Visitors can walk a one-mile trail, attend animal feedings and training sessions, and learn about wildlife conservation.
attractions.hours: Seasonal (March through November): Sunday 10am-4:30pm, Monday CLOSED, Tuesday-Friday 10am-4:30pm, Saturday 10am-4:30pm. November weekends: 11am-3pm. Closed during winter (December through March, following Thanksgiving).
attractions.days_open: Tuesday-Sunday (Closed Mondays and winter season December-March)
attractions.admission: Adult: $20.00 | Senior (65+): $16.00 | Child (under 12): $12.00 | Infant (2 and under): Free
highlights: Home to 40+ species of rescued wildlife including cougars, wolves, bears, and tigers
One-mile walking trail through natural habitat enclosures
Daily animal feeding and training demonstrations at 10:30am, 11:30am, 1:30pm, and 2:30pm
Meet and Greet experiences with select animals
Nonprofit organization dedicated to wildlife conservation and education
Picnic and playground area for families
attractions.tips: Visit early in the day when animals are most active, especially for nocturnal predators. Wear comfortable walking shoes as the trail is compact dirt and decomposed granite. Pets are not allowed. Bring a picnic for the large picnic area. Docents are available throughout the day with questions. Golf cart rentals available for disabled/handicapped/elderly visitors with advance reservation (call 775-475-9626). The facility is about 30 minutes outside Reno with limited cell service. Check social media for weather-related closures.
attractions.nearby: Peavine Mountain, Sparks Marina Park, Reno-Sparks Convention Center, Nevada Museum of Art, National Automobile Museum, University of Nevada Reno

attractions.name: Artown Festival
attractions.description: Artown is Reno's premier month-long summer arts festival held every July, featuring nearly 500 events produced by more than 100 organizations at over 130 locations city and countywide. Since 1996, Artown has been inspiring community through the arts, showcasing multidisciplinary performances including music, dance, theater, visual arts, and literary events. It is the only multidisciplinary arts festival of its kind on the West Coast and has been recognized by the National Endowment for the Arts as one of the most comprehensive arts festivals in the country.
attractions.hours: Varies by event — events run throughout the entire month of July at different times and locations across Reno. Check artown.org for the full event schedule.
attractions.days_open: Throughout July (month-long festival, dates vary by event)
attractions.admission: Varies by event — many events are free, while some ticketed events range in price. Check artown.org for specific event pricing.
highlights: Nearly 500 events over 31 days across 130+ locations
Multidisciplinary arts including music, dance, theater, visual arts, and literary events
The only multidisciplinary arts festival of its kind on the West Coast
Running since 1996, celebrating over 30 years of arts in Reno
Featured headliner events like Ballets Jazz Montréal at Pioneer Center
Supported by National Endowment for the Arts and Nevada Arts Council
attractions.tips: Check artown.org early for the full July event calendar and create your own personalized schedule using the "My Events" feature. Many events are free, but popular ticketed headliners sell out quickly. Events take place throughout Reno and Washoe County, so plan your transportation accordingly. Follow Artown on social media (@renoisartown) for last-minute updates and event changes.
attractions.nearby: Downtown Reno, Pioneer Center for the Performing Arts, Nevada Museum of Art, National Automobile Museum, Reno Riverwalk District, McKinley Arts and Culture Center

attractions.name: Atlantis Casino Resort Spa
attractions.description: Nestled at the base of the majestic Sierra Nevada, Atlantis Casino Resort Spa is a AAA Four Diamond resort located in the heart of Reno's restaurant, shopping, and entertainment district. The property features a full-service spa, casino gaming, award-winning dining, and luxurious accommodations. It is ranked as TripAdvisor's #1 Reno casino hotel.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year (casino and resort). Spa Atlantis: Daily 8:00am - 9:00pm. Atlantis Salon: Daily 10:00am - 6:00pm. Fitness Center: Daily 6:00am - 10:00pm. Pools & Cabanas: Daily 6:00am - 10:00pm. Fun Center Arcade: Sunday-Thursday 10:00am - 10:00pm, Friday-Saturday 10:00am - Midnight.
attractions.days_open: 24 hours a day, 7 days a week
attractions.admission: Free to visit the resort and casino. Restaurant meals, room accommodations, spa services, and entertainment are priced individually.
highlights: AAA Four Diamond resort with luxury rooms and suites
Full-service Spa Atlantis with 15 treatment rooms, saunas, and therapy areas
Casino gaming including Blackjack, Craps, Baccarat, Roulette, Pai Gow Poker, and Race & Sports Book
More than a dozen dining options including Bistro Napa and Social Hour
Fun Center Arcade with games like Mario Kart and prize redemption
Indoor and outdoor pools with cabanas and fitness center
attractions.tips: The resort is free to enter and explore — you don't need to be a hotel guest to enjoy the casino, restaurants, or shops. Visit Bistro Napa's Social Hour from 4-6pm daily for affordable small plates, sliders, flatbread pizzas, and drinks. The spa has an experimental shower room (skip it if you prefer). The Self-Service Prize Hub at the Fun Center is open 24/7.
attractions.nearby: Eldorado Resort Casino, Silver Legacy Resort Casino, Reno Arch, National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Riverwalk District

attractions.name: Bartley Ranch Regional Park
attractions.description: Bartley Ranch Regional Park is a 56-acre regional park in south Reno featuring scenic pastures, multi-use trails, horse arenas, and a collection of historic ranch buildings and equipment. The park is home to the Western Heritage Interpretive Center, the Historic Huffaker School, and the outdoor Robert Z. Hawkins Amphitheater which hosts concerts and community events. Originally a working ranch until 1985, it opened as a public park in 1995 and now serves as a preservation site for Truckee Meadows ranching history.
attractions.hours: Spring: 8:00 a.m. – 7:00 p.m. (First day of Spring time change to the Friday of Memorial Day Weekend) | Summer: 8:00 a.m. – 9:00 p.m. (Memorial Day weekend through Labor Day) | Fall: 8:00 a.m. – 7:00 p.m. (Day after Labor Day to Fall time change) | Winter: 8:00 a.m. – 5:00 p.m. (Fall time change to Spring time change)
attractions.days_open: Open daily, sunrise to sunset (seasonal hours as listed)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Robert Z. Hawkins Amphitheater for concerts and performances
Western Heritage Interpretive Center with ranching history exhibits
Historic Huffaker Schoolhouse
Multi-use trails including 1.5-mile Ranch Loop Trail, 0.4-mile Quail Run Trail, and 0.2-mile Flume Trail
Horse arenas and 56 acres of scenic pasture
Reservable group picnic pavilions (Flying B, Huffaker, Last Chance, Plaza)
attractions.tips: Note that the park does NOT have a playground and is NOT a dog park — pets must be kept on leash. There is little shade, so avoid visiting on very hot days. GPS may not always lead directly to the park entrance; look for the covered bridge on Bartley Ranch Road. RV parking is available in the left lot upon entry but there are no shower or dump facilities. The park is popular for events, so check the calendar for concerts at the amphitheater.
attractions.nearby: Atlantis Casino Resort Spa, Peppermill Casino, National Automobile Museum, Virginia Lake Park, Truckee River Walk, Reno Riverwalk District, Nevada Museum of Art, Greater Nevada Field, Rancho San Rafael Regional Park, University of Nevada Reno

attractions.name: BaseCamp Reno (Whitney Peak Hotel Climbing Wall)
attractions.description: BaseCamp Reno is home to the world's tallest artificial climbing wall at 164 feet, certified by the Guinness Book of World Records. Located on the exterior of the Whitney Peak Hotel in downtown Reno, it features a 7,000 square foot indoor bouldering park with a separate kids' room, plus outdoor climbing on the hotel's facade. The facility also includes a full gym, steam room, and sauna, making it a unique urban climbing destination.
attractions.address: 255 N Virginia St, Whitney Peak Hotel, Reno, NV 89501
attractions.hours: Monday-Friday: 8:00 AM - 10:00 PM; Saturday-Sunday: 8:00 AM - 9:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adult day pass (drop-in): $17; Adult (hotel guest): $10; Senior (55+) / Youth (14 & under) / Student (drop-in): $12; Senior / Youth / Student (hotel guest): $5; Big Wall outdoor access (non-members): $40; Big Wall outdoor access (members): $35. Membership options and family bundles available.
highlights: World's tallest artificial climbing wall at 164 feet (Guinness World Record certified)
7,000 square foot indoor bouldering park with over 2,900 square feet of climbable space
Dedicated kids' climbing area with safe, fun features
Full gym facility with steam room and sauna
IFSC certified 15m speed wall and 70' top rope/lead climbing wall
Auto-belay devices on 35' walls for beginners
attractions.tips: Hotel guests receive significant discounts on day passes ($10 vs $17 for adults). Outdoor Big Wall climbing costs extra ($40 non-member). Wear comfortable athletic clothing. Consider visiting on weekdays for smaller crowds. The indoor bouldering area is great for beginners, while the outdoor wall is for more experienced climbers.
attractions.nearby: Reno Arch, National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Riverwalk District, Eldorado Resort Casino, Silver Legacy Resort Casino, Circus Circus Reno, Midtown District, Truckee River Whitewater Park, Discovery Museum

attractions.name: Eldorado Resort Casino
attractions.description: Eldorado Resort Casino is a hotel and casino located in Downtown Reno, Nevada, owned and operated by Caesars Entertainment. It anchors a network of connected hotel-casinos in the downtown Reno core, including Circus Circus Reno and Silver Legacy Reno. The property features an 81,000 square foot casino floor with slots, table games, and a race and sportsbook, plus luxury hotel rooms, diverse dining, and nonstop entertainment.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily
attractions.admission: Free (casino floor access is free; hotel rooms and dining available at varying prices)
highlights: 81,000 square foot casino floor with slots, table games, and race & sportsbook
Connected to Silver Legacy Reno and Circus Circus Reno via indoor walkways
Luxury hotel rooms and suites with downtown Reno views
Award-winning dining options and nightlife
Caesars Rewards loyalty program
24-hour entertainment and gaming
attractions.tips: The Eldorado is connected via indoor walkways to Silver Legacy and Circus Circus, allowing easy access to multiple casinos without going outside. Parking is available on-site. Check in time is 3:00 PM and check out is 12:00 PM. The casino is within walking distance of the Truckee River and downtown Reno attractions.
attractions.nearby: Silver Legacy Resort Casino (connected), Circus Circus Reno (connected), Truckee River, National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Reno Arch, Riverwalk District, Harrah's Reno

attractions.name: Fleischmann Planetarium & Science Center
attractions.description: The Fleischmann Planetarium and Science Center is located on the University of Nevada, Reno campus and features a 60-seat Full-Dome Theater, an Exhibit Hall, and a Science Store. Opened in 1963, it was the first planetarium in the United States to feature an atmospherium — a dome-shaped theater that could project weather phenomena. It serves as Northern Nevada's premier destination for space education and astronomy.
attractions.hours: Friday: 3:30 PM - 8:00 PM; Saturday: 9:30 AM - 9:00 PM; Sunday: 2:30 PM - 7:00 PM; Monday-Thursday: Closed to public (available for field trips Mon-Sat 10 AM - 1 PM by appointment)
attractions.days_open: Friday, Saturday, Sunday (public); Monday-Saturday (field trips by appointment)
attractions.admission: Adult: $15 per person (show); Student/Senior: $10 per person (show); Walk-in (exhibits only, no show): $3 per person; Science Store browsing: Free; Members: Free tickets to all public screenings
highlights: 60-seat Full-Dome Theater with immersive astronomy shows
Exhibit Hall featuring spaceflight, solar system, and meteorite displays
Science Store with educational gifts and space-themed merchandise
Monthly rotating shows and First Friday live music concerts in the dome
Stargazing events on Saturday and Sunday evenings
Hands-on workshops on Second Saturdays
attractions.tips: Arrive at least 15-30 minutes before a show to get the best seats, especially for popular weekend showtimes. Parking is available on the third floor of the West Stadium Parking Complex with validation. The walk-in fee of $3 is a great option if you just want to explore the exhibits without a show. Check the current show schedule online as shows rotate monthly.
attractions.nearby: University of Nevada, Reno campus, Wilbur D. May Center, National Automobile Museum, W. M. Keck Earth Science and Mineral Engineering Museum, Greater Nevada Field, Nevada Historical Society, Downtown Reno Arch

attractions.name: Galena Creek Regional Park
attractions.description: Galena Creek Regional Park is Washoe County's oldest regional park, purchased in 1931, nestled in a forested area on the eastern slope of the Sierra Nevada seven miles up Mt. Rose Highway from I-580. The park offers year-round recreation including hiking along Galena, Jones, and White's Creeks in summer, and cross-country skiing, snowshoeing, and sledding in winter. Facilities include the Galena Creek Visitor Center, Marilyn's Pond for fishing, group picnic areas, and a fish hatchery.
attractions.address: 18250 Mt. Rose Highway, Reno, NV 89511 (North Entrance); 18350 Mt. Rose Highway, Reno, NV 89511 (South Entrance)
attractions.hours: Summer (May 1–Oct 15): Daily 8:00 AM – 7:00 PM. Winter (Oct 16–April 30): Daily 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM. Visitor Center: Summer Tue–Sun 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM; Winter Fri–Sun 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM.
attractions.admission: Free. No fees for park entry or visitor center. Group picnic areas require reservations and fees.
highlights: Marilyn's Pond – ADA accessible fishing pond stocked with Rainbow trout
Galena Creek Visitor Center – interpretive exhibits, gift shop, and nature programs
Hiking trails along Galena, Jones, and White's Creeks with varying difficulty
Winter activities including cross-country skiing, snowshoeing, and sledding
Group picnic areas (Bearmat, Jones Creek, Manzanita) available for reservation
Galena Creek Fish Hatchery
attractions.tips: Call (775) 849-2511 for current weather and trail conditions before visiting. This is bear country – store food and trash properly. Bring your own snowshoes in winter or borrow free ones from the Visitor Center. A valid Nevada fishing license is required for anglers over age 12 at Marilyn's Pond. The south entrance offers easier access for families with children and the fishing pond.
attractions.nearby: Mt. Rose Ski Resort (2.4 mi), Phillip & Annie Callahan Park (2 mi), Bowers Mansion Regional Park, Davis Creek Regional Park, Galena Creek Visitor Center (0.8 mi)

attractions.name: Grand Sierra Resort
attractions.description: Grand Sierra Resort is a premier casino and hotel resort located in the heart of Reno, Nevada. It features Reno's largest gaming floor, multiple dining options including the acclaimed Chickie's & Pete's sports bar, and hosts major entertainment acts at the Grand Theatre. The resort offers luxurious hotel rooms and suites, a full-service spa, two pools, and the Infinity Rewards loyalty program shared with SAHARA Las Vegas.
attractions.hours: Casino: Open 24 hours. Dining venues vary by restaurant. Spa hours vary. Pools are seasonal.
attractions.admission: Free entry to casino and public areas. Hotel room rates vary by date and room type. Entertainment show tickets priced individually.
highlights: Reno's largest gaming floor with extensive slot machines and table games
Grand Theatre hosting major concerts and entertainment acts
Multiple dining options including Chickie's & Pete's sports bar
Full-service spa and wellness center
Two outdoor pools with bars and food trucks
Infinity Rewards loyalty program with SAHARA Las Vegas
attractions.tips: Join the free Infinity Rewards program to earn points and comps. The casino is open 24/7. Check the entertainment calendar for upcoming shows at the Grand Theatre. The resort has ample parking and is close to the Reno-Sparks Convention Center.
attractions.nearby: Reno-Sparks Convention Center, National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Truckee River Walk, Atlantis Casino Resort, Peppermill Resort Spa Casino, Victorian Square, Reno Arch

attractions.name: Greater Nevada Field
attractions.description: Greater Nevada Field is a Minor League Baseball venue located in downtown Reno, Nevada, on the north bank of the Truckee River. Opened on April 17, 2009, it is the home of the Triple-A Reno Aces, the Pacific Coast League affiliate of the Arizona Diamondbacks. The 9,100-capacity stadium welcomes over 500,000 ticketed fans per year and features views of surrounding mountains, rivers, trains, and casinos.
attractions.hours: Game-dependent. Gates typically open 1 hour before game time. Seasonal events (Reno Frightfest, City of Reno Ice Rink) have separate hours. Contact venue for specific event times.
attractions.days_open: Open 365 days a year for baseball games and special events
attractions.admission: Ticket prices vary by game and seating area. Single-game tickets typically range from approximately $10–$40 depending on seat location. Group tickets, season tickets, and memberships also available. Check [Reno Aces website](https://www.milb.com/reno/tickets) for current pricing.
highlights: Home of the Reno Aces, Triple-A affiliate of the Arizona Diamondbacks
9,100-capacity stadium with 22 luxury skyboxes and a 150-person club suite
Located on the north bank of the Truckee River in downtown Reno
Hosts special events including Reno Frightfest and the City of Reno Ice Rink
Part of the Freight House District redevelopment with nearby dining and entertainment
Hosted the 2013 Triple-A All-Star Game
attractions.tips: Arrive early to explore the Freight House District for food and drinks before the game. Parking is available in a 9-story garage behind the stadium (flat rate ~$10) or in nearby downtown casino parking garages. Thirsty Thursdays offer drink specials. Check the promotional schedule for themed nights like Mickey Mouse Night and Fireworks Friday. The venue is within walking distance of downtown Reno casinos, restaurants, and nightlife.
attractions.nearby: Downtown Reno casinos (Eldorado, Silver Legacy, Circus Circus), Truckee River Walk, National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Reno Arch, Riverwalk District dining and entertainment

attractions.name: Hot August Nights
attractions.description: Hot August Nights is the largest nostalgic car show in the world, celebrating America's love affair with classic cars, rock and roll, and nights filled with nostalgia. Now in its 40th anniversary year, the event spans 10 days with classic car cruises, drag races, show-n-shines, and free nightly concerts featuring top-name entertainment. The festival takes place across 10 venues in the Reno-Sparks area including downtown Reno, the University of Nevada, and Sparks.
attractions.address: Various venues across Reno and Sparks (Main event hosted by Caesars Entertainment's Reno Resorts in Downtown Reno)
attractions.hours: Event-specific hours vary by venue and activity. Main car participant registration runs July 31, 2026 at 8:00am through August 9, 2026 at 1:00pm. Nightly concerts typically begin at 8:15 PM. Show-n-Shines and cruises run during daytime hours. Drag races run on select dates. Exact schedules vary by event and venue.
attractions.admission: Most events are FREE to the public, including nightly concerts and street cruises. Drag Races Spectator General Admission: $20 (all day). Car participant registration fees apply for those showing vehicles. Specific ticket prices vary by event tier.
highlights: Largest nostalgic car show in the world with up to 6,000 classic car participants
40th Anniversary Celebration in 2026 spanning 10 days
Free nightly concerts featuring top-name entertainment at multiple venues
Drag races presented by Amsoil at the Nugget Casino Resort
Show-n-Shines and street cruises through downtown Reno and Sparks
Events at 10 venues including University of Nevada, Reno and Nugget Casino Resort
attractions.tips: Most events are free to spectate — arrive early for popular concerts to get good viewing spots. If you're a car participant, register online before July 1, 2026 as spots fill up quickly (6,000 limit). Check the event schedule on the official website for exact times and locations as schedules are subject to change. Dress warmly for evening outdoor events as Reno nights can be cool in August. Parking can be challenging downtown — consider using nearby casino parking or public transit.
attractions.nearby: University of Nevada, Reno; Reno-Sparks Convention Center; Atlantis Casino Resort Spa; Peppermill Reno Resort Spa Casino; J Resort; Nugget Casino Resort (Sparks); Whitney Peak Hotel; Reno-Sparks Livestock Events Center

attractions.name: Hunter Creek Trail
attractions.description: Hunter Creek Trail is a 5.8-mile out-and-back hiking trail located in the foothills of the Eastern Sierra, just minutes from downtown Reno. The trail features diverse ecosystems ranging from desert sage to shaded pine groves, culminating in a spectacular 30-40 foot waterfall near the 3-mile mark. With 1,200 feet of elevation gain, it offers a moderate-to-strenuous hike with panoramic views of Reno and the surrounding mountains.
attractions.address: Michael D. Thompson Trailhead, Woodchuck Circle
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily (trailhead accessible year-round)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 30-40 foot waterfall at the end of the trail
Diverse ecosystems from desert sage to pine groves
Panoramic views of Reno and the Eastern Sierra
Water and bathroom facilities at the trailhead
Only 20 minutes from downtown Reno
5.8-mile out-and-back trail with 1,200 ft elevation gain
attractions.tips: Start early in the morning to beat the heat and secure parking, especially in summer. Bring plenty of water (the first 2/3 of the trail is exposed with little shade). Wear good hiking boots for rocky sections and stream crossings. Use hiking poles for balance on loose rock and stream crossings. Allow 3-4 hours round trip. Best visited April through October.
attractions.nearby: Mayberry Park, Truckee River Walk, National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Idlewild Park, Bartley Ranch Regional Park, Galena Creek Regional Park, Downtown Reno, Virginia Lake Park, Greater Nevada Field

attractions.name: Idlewild Park
attractions.description: Idlewild Park is a large, 49-acre community park close to downtown Reno, Nevada on the Truckee River. It includes a rose garden, duck ponds, two children's parks, a pool, and a skate park. It holds the Reno Earth Day celebration every year and hosts the Reno Street Food food truck event through the summer months. It is also home to the historic California Building on Cowan Drive.
attractions.hours: Park: 5:00 AM - 10:00 PM daily; Train: Tuesday-Friday 11:00 AM-3:00 PM, Saturday-Sunday 11:00 AM-4:00 PM, Monday closed
attractions.admission: Free (pool has separate fee; rentable shelters available for a fee)
highlights: 49-acre community park along the Truckee River
Historic California Building - Spanish-style architecture hosting cultural events
Rose Garden with 200 varieties and over 1,750 roses
Two children's parks, skate park, and pool
Duck ponds and water play toys
Reno Earth Day celebration and Reno Street Food food truck events
attractions.tips: Best time to visit is 8:00 AM - 5:00 PM. The train is cash only. The Rose Garden is best in early June through summer. Parking is available along Idlewild Drive and Cowan Street. Bring cash for the train tickets.
attractions.nearby: Midtown Reno district, Truckee River bike path, Newlands Natural Spring Park, Discovery Museum, Wilbur D. May Museum, Rancho San Rafael Park

attractions.name: MidTown Reno
attractions.description: MidTown Reno is the city's premier artistic and cultural hub, spanning about 1.2 miles of South Virginia Street in central Reno. It is home to over 250 locally owned businesses including boutiques, restaurants, breweries, tattoo parlors, and coffee shops. The district features vibrant murals, street art, and historical plaques, making it a thriving community gathering space for locals and visitors alike.
attractions.hours: N/A — MidTown Reno is an open-air district with no set hours. Individual businesses within the district have their own operating hours.
attractions.days_open: Open daily (district is accessible 24/7; business hours vary by establishment)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Over 250 locally owned businesses including boutiques, restaurants, bars, and coffee shops
Vibrant murals and street art covering building walls throughout the district
14 MidTown Historical Plaques highlighting the area's history
MidTown Welcome Center located inside Reno Public Market at 299 E. Plumb Ln
Community events including Dancing in the Streets and Glowing in the Streets 5K
Named a Nevada Main Street community, supporting local business revitalization
attractions.tips: MidTown is an open-air district, so explore on foot or by bike to fully experience the murals and storefronts. The district roughly spans from Liberty Street south to Plumb Lane along South Virginia Street, extending a few blocks east and west. Visit during daytime for the best lighting to photograph the colorful murals. The MidTown Welcome Center inside Reno Public Market is a good starting point for maps and information.
attractions.nearby: Reno Public Market, Truckee River Walk, Downtown Reno, Nevada Museum of Art, Terry Lee Wells Nevada Discovery Museum, Peppermill Resort

attractions.name: National Automobile Museum
attractions.description: The National Automobile Museum in Reno, Nevada, houses more than 225 vintage and historic automobiles displayed in authentic street scenes with period facades and sounds. The collection primarily features vehicles from the legendary Bill Harrah collection and includes cars owned by celebrities such as James Dean, Frank Sinatra, John Wayne, and Elvis Presley. Located along the Truckee River in downtown Reno, the museum is consistently rated as the #1 thing to do in Reno by TripAdvisor.
attractions.hours: Monday – Friday: 9 am to 5 pm; Saturday: 10 am to 4 pm; Sunday: 10 am to 4 pm. Closed on Thanksgiving, Christmas Eve, and Christmas Day.
attractions.days_open: Open 7 days a week (closed Thanksgiving, Christmas Eve, and Christmas Day)
attractions.admission: General Admission: $16 (ages 18-64); Senior (65+): $15; Active Military, Veterans, First Responders (with ID): $14; Youth (6-17): $13; Children (5 and younger): $3; Members: Free; Parking: Free. Annual membership starts at $45.
highlights: Over 225 historic automobiles displayed in authentic 1920s-1940s street scenes with period facades and sounds
Celebrity Automobiles collection featuring cars owned by James Dean, Frank Sinatra, John Wayne, Elvis Presley, and Lana Turner
1907 Thomas Flyer 35, winner of the 1908 New York to Paris Race
Guided museum tours offered daily at 1:30 pm based on volunteer availability
Rated #1 thing to do in Reno by TripAdvisor (2022 Travelers' Choice)
Champion Vehicles and Limited Edition Cars exhibits including rare dragsters, race cars, and experimental vehicles
attractions.tips: Free self-guided audio tours are available. Guided tours are offered daily at 1:30 pm based on volunteer availability. Photography is allowed and encouraged — don't forget your camera. Free parking is available at the museum lot. Allow 1.5–2 hours to fully explore all exhibits. The museum also features a theater with presentations about automotive history.
attractions.nearby: Reno Arch, Truckee River Walk, Nevada Museum of Art, Terry Lee Wells Nevada Discovery Museum (The Discovery), Reno-Tahoe International Airport, Eldorado Resort Casino, Peppermill Reno, Walking distance to downtown Reno restaurants and entertainment.

attractions.name: National Bowling Stadium
attractions.description: The National Bowling Stadium is a 363,000-square-foot ten-pin bowling stadium in Reno, Nevada, recognizable for its iconic 80-foot aluminum geodesic sphere. Built in 1995, it is one of the largest bowling facilities in the world and hosts major professional bowling tournaments. The stadium primarily operates during tournament events and is not regularly open to the general public, though it occasionally opens for charity events and special occasions.
attractions.hours: Wednesday–Sunday: 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM (when hosting events/tournaments; not regularly open to the public)
attractions.days_open: Wednesday through Sunday (event-dependent; closed most days)
attractions.admission: Not regularly open to the public. During occasional public events: approximately $50 per lane (up to 5 people) or $15 per person for three games including shoe rental. Tournament ticket prices vary by event.
highlights: Iconic 80-foot geodesic aluminum sphere atop the building
363,000 square feet of bowling space
Hosts major professional bowling tournaments including PBA events
178-seat iWerks theater (limited availability)
Features the National Bowling Hall of Fame
Located in downtown Reno with attached parking garage
attractions.tips: The National Bowling Stadium is NOT regularly open to the public — it primarily hosts tournaments. Call ahead at (775) 335-8840 or check their website/social media to confirm if open bowling is available on your desired date. The stadium occasionally opens for charity events like the Eddy Bowl fundraiser. For recreational bowling, consider other alleys in Reno.
attractions.nearby: Downtown Reno casinos (Eldorado, Silver Legacy, Circus Circus), National Automobile Museum, Nevada Museum of Art, Reno Arch, Truckee River Walk, Discovery Museum, Bigelow-Benedetti Art Center, Idlewild Park

attractions.name: Neon Line District
attractions.description: The Neon Line District is a revitalized multi-block entertainment district on West 4th Street in downtown Reno, Nevada. It features a half-mile-long installation of historic neon tribute signage combined with LED-lit streetscapes, rotating Burning Man art sculptures, live concert events, festivals, and new destinations to work, live, and play. The district is anchored by J Resort and includes sister property Gold Dust West, J Resort's Glow Plaza, and Renova Flats living space.
attractions.hours: Open 24/7 (outdoor public district)
attractions.admission: Free (outdoor public district; individual venues, events, and businesses within the district may have their own pricing)
highlights: Half-mile-long installation of historic neon tribute signage
Nine restored historic neon signs along West 4th Street
Rotating Burning Man art sculptures (Desert Guard, Rearing Horse, Bloom, SQUARED)
J Resort and Gold Dust West casino resorts
LED-lit streetscape and glow plaza
attractions.tips: Visit in the early evening or after dark to experience the neon lights at their best. The district is an ongoing development project spanning 20 city blocks from West Street to Keystone Avenue and Interstate 80 to West Second Street. Art installations rotate every two years, so each visit may feature different sculptures. The area is walkable and connects to other downtown Reno districts.
attractions.nearby: J Resort Casino, Gold Dust West Casino, Downtown Reno Arch District, Reno Riverwalk District, Reno Brewery District, Freight House District, University of Nevada Reno (UNR)

attractions.name: Nevada Historical Society
attractions.description: The Nevada Historical Society is a museum dedicated to preserving and presenting the history and culture of Nevada. Located on the University of Nevada, Reno campus, it houses extensive collections including approximately 500,000 photographs, artifacts, and documents spanning the state's history from 1862 to the present. The museum features exhibits on Nevada's mining heritage, Native American culture, and the American Gaming Archives. The building is currently closed for seismic retrofitting and renovations through December 2026, with off-site programming available.

Nevada Opera was named one of the "Best of Reno" by Reno Magazine, celebrating decades of contributions to the community's cultural arts scene.

attractions.name: Oxbow Nature Study Area
attractions.description: The Oxbow Nature Study Area is a 22-acre urban nature preserve in the heart of Reno, jointly managed by the Nevada Department of Wildlife and the City of Reno. This riparian conservation area features wetlands, cottonwoods, willows, and a small pond alongside the Truckee River, offering a serene escape from the city. Visitors can explore a 1-mile ADA-accessible nature trail with interpretive signs, viewing platforms, and abundant wildlife viewing opportunities.
attractions.hours: June through October: 8:00 AM to sunset. November through May: 8:00 AM to 4:00 PM (parking gate closes at 4 PM).

Interpretive signs and viewing platforms overlooking marshes and river

Small visitor center/interpretive building with educational programs

attractions.tips: No dogs or bicycles are allowed in the park to protect wildlife habitat. Bring binoculars for birdwatching. The visitor center has sporadic hours, so don't rely on it being open. Mornings are the coolest time to visit, especially in summer.

attractions.name: Rancho San Rafael Regional Park
attractions.description: At 580 acres, Rancho San Rafael Regional Park is Washoe County's largest and most dynamic park, offering a wide variety of recreational activities and facilities. The park features the Wilbur D. May Center (museum and arboretum), disc golf course, dog park, volleyball courts, playgrounds, picnic areas, hiking and biking trails, and the Reno Softball Complex. The park is administered by Washoe County Regional Parks and Open Space with no entrance or parking fees.

attractions.name: Reno Arch - The Biggest Little City in the World
attractions.description: The Reno Arch is an iconic landmark spanning Virginia Street at the intersection with Commercial Row in downtown Reno, Nevada. Built in 1926 to commemorate the completion of the Lincoln and Victory Highways, it is the third version of the arch and displays Reno's famous motto, "The Biggest Little City in the World." The current arch, installed in 1987 and updated with silver and blue lights in 2018, serves as the welcoming gateway to downtown Reno and is one of the city's most recognizable symbols.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours a day (outdoor landmark accessible at all times)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Displays Reno's famous motto: 'The Biggest Little City in the World'

Iconic silver and blue LED lights honoring the University of Nevada, Reno Wolfpack

Featured in numerous movies and a defining symbol of Reno
Historic landmark with over 95 years of history
attractions.tips: Visit at night to see the arch illuminated with its colorful LED lights — it's most spectacular after dark. The arch is easily walkable from nearby casinos and restaurants along Virginia Street. Great photo opportunities looking up from underneath the arch or from a distance with the city lights behind it. Street-closing events and festivals are frequently held in the Arch District throughout the year.

The Reno Phil performs primarily at the Pioneer Center for the Performing Arts and offers special events like live film screenings with orchestral accompaniment and free summer concert series.

It hosts the "Wildest, Richest Rodeo in the West" — a 10-day PRCA sanctioned event drawing over 140,000 spectators annually. attractions.name: River Inn (formerly Lawton's Hot Springs)
attractions.description: The River Inn, originally known as Lawton's Hot Springs, is a historic abandoned casino resort located on the Truckee River in Reno, Nevada. Founded in 1884 by Sumner Laughton, it operated as a hot springs resort, later becoming a casino and motel before closing in 1978. The A-frame casino structure built in the early 1980s remains standing but has never opened, and the property is now privately owned and fenced off, occasionally used for SWAT training.

Celebrating motorcycle culture and the spirit of the open road, this vibrant gathering attracts over 50,000 motorcycle enthusiasts and music lovers, featuring live performances, custom bike shows, scenic rides, and vendor booths. With its stunning backdrop of the Sierra Nevada mountains, it is considered one of the largest motorcycle rallies in the nation.

attractions.name: The Riverwalk District

The district centers around the scenic Truckee River corridor and includes the Truckee River Whitewater Park, Wingfield Park, and numerous public art installations. Named one of "America's Top 10 Coolest Riverwalks," it is a hub for dining, shopping, arts, culture, and outdoor recreation in the heart of the Biggest Little City.

The monthly Reno Wine Walk (third Saturday) is a popular event — purchase a tasting ticket and wine glass at any participating merchant for free refills. The area runs from Idlewild Park in the west to Wingfield Park in the east, so comfortable walking shoes are recommended. The Truckee River Whitewater Park offers kayaking and tubing in warmer months.

attractions.description: The Thomas Creek Trail is a 5.5-mile out-and-back trail on the southwest side of Reno, Nevada, providing access to the Mt. Rose Wilderness. It connects with the Rim to Reno Trail for longer trips within the Mt. Rose Wilderness area. The trailhead is popular with day hikers, equestrians, and mountain bikers.

attractions.hours: Open 24 hours/day, January - December
attractions.admission: Free

Scenic views of the Sierra Nevada foothills
Access to Jones Creek and Whites Creek trail systems
attractions.tips: Dogs must be leashed at the trailhead and for the first mile on the trail. No potable water is available at the site, so bring your own. Vault toilets are available at the trailhead. The access road (Timberline Drive) may have seasonal closures, so check conditions before visiting. From Reno, take I-580 west to Mt. Rose Highway (SR-431), drive approximately 4.5 miles, then turn right onto N. Timberline Drive and travel about 1.3 miles to the trailhead.

Located in the heart of downtown Reno, it is open year-round and free to the public.

attractions.description: The Wilbur D. May Museum is located in Rancho San Rafael Regional Park in northwest Reno, Nevada. It presents the personal collection of adventurer Wilbur D. May from more than 40 trips made around the world, including artifacts from South America, American Western art, Japanese swords, European glasswork, Egyptian tomb relics, and African wildlife trophies. The museum is housed in a ranch-style building designed to look like Wilbur's home, which was originally located on his private 1,800-acre ranch. It also features an arboretum and botanical garden right next to the museum.
attractions.address: 1595 North Sierra Street (Rancho San Rafael Regional Park), Reno, NV 89503
attractions.hours: Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Thursday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Friday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Saturday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM; Sunday: 12:00 PM - 4:00 PM; Monday: Closed; Tuesday: Closed
attractions.admission: Adults: $7.00; Seniors (62+): $5.00; Children (3-17): $5.00; Children under 3: Free
highlights: Personal collection from 40+ worldwide trips including artifacts from South America, Africa, and Asia
Permanent exhibits featuring ivory figurines, trophy game animals, Tang Dynasty pottery, and shrunken heads
Ranch-style building designed to resemble Wilbur D. May's original home on his 1,800-acre private ranch
Adjacent Wilbur D. May Arboretum and Botanical Garden with lush year-round strolling paths
Rotating exhibits for the whole family with smaller art showcases
Museum, gift shop, arboretum, and event venue all in one location
attractions.tips: Visit the adjacent Wilbur D. May Arboretum and Botanical Garden, which is free to visit and offers a peaceful stroll year-round. Plan to spend 1-2 hours at the museum to fully explore the permanent collections. The museum is located within Rancho San Rafael Regional Park, so consider combining your visit with a picnic or walk in the park. Check for special events or the fall lantern festival when the arboretum has extended hours and admission fees may apply at night.
attractions.nearby: Rancho San Rafael Regional Park, Wilbur D. May Arboretum and Botanical Garden, Terry Lee Wells Nevada Discovery Museum (The Discovery), Nevada Museum of Art, Reno-Sparks Convention Center, Downtown Reno casinos and entertainment district

attractions.name: Wingfield Park
attractions.description: Wingfield Park is a recreational park located in downtown Reno along the Truckee River. It features an amphitheater for concerts and events, kayaking and swimming areas in the Reno Whitewater Park, and scenic pathways along the river. The park hosts major events including the Reno River Festival and Artown activities in July.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to Sunset (typical park hours; specific hours may vary by season)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Amphitheater for concerts and live performances
Reno Whitewater Park for kayaking and swimming
Scenic pathways along the Truckee River
Hosts Reno River Festival and Artown events
Grassy areas and picnic spots with river views
Located in the heart of downtown Reno near casinos and restaurants
attractions.tips: The park may be closed during the Arlington Bridge replacement project (expected to resume in 2026). Check for local events, especially during Artown in July. Parking is available via meters on 1st Street or the nearby Parking Gallery structure; free parking is available after 5 PM on weekdays at the Washoe County lot on Virginia and Court Streets. Bring a life jacket if kayaking in the whitewater park.
attractions.nearby: Truckee River Walk, Reno Arch, Nevada Museum of Art, National Automobile Museum, Pioneer Center for the Performing Arts, Barbara Bennett Park, Lake Mansion, McCarran Mansion

attractions.name: John Ascuaga's Nugget Casino Resort
attractions.description: John Ascuaga's Nugget Casino Resort is a classic hotel and casino located in the heart of Victorian Square in Sparks, Nevada. It features vintage casino charm, award-winning dining, multiple entertainment venues, and a wide variety of slot machines and table games. The resort offers fully renovated rooms and suites, a casino tower, and hosts live shows, concerts, and events.
attractions.hours: Open 24 Hours
attractions.admission: Free (no admission fee to enter the casino; gaming requires minimum bets; hotel rooms, dining, and shows are separately priced)
highlights: Vintage casino charm with classic Nevada atmosphere
Award-winning dining including Anthony's Chophouse and Oyster Bar
Live entertainment, concerts, and comedy shows
Winners' Zone rewards program with 5 tiers of benefits
Multiple slot machines, table games, live poker, and sports wagering
Fully renovated hotel rooms and suites
attractions.tips: The casino is open 24 hours daily. The Winners' Zone rewards card is free to join and offers perks like free play, comp dollars, and VIP benefits. Check the events calendar for upcoming concerts and shows. The resort is located in Victorian Square, walking distance from other Sparks attractions.
attractions.nearby: Reno-Sparks Convention Center, National Automobile Museum, Sparks Marina, Atlantis Casino Resort Spa, Reno's Riverwalk District

attractions.name: Sparks Heritage Museum (Sparks Museum & Cultural Center)
attractions.description: Located in the heart of historic downtown Sparks, Nevada, the Sparks Heritage Museum boasts 4,000 square feet of indoor exhibit space featuring a permanent gallery highlighting the history and heritage of the city of Sparks, along with changing galleries featuring historic, artistic, and cultural themes. The museum also maintains an outdoor train exhibit and the historic one-room Glendale Schoolhouse. Using displays and artifacts, the exhibits connect the present with life in the past and provide insights into the rapid changes northern Nevada has undergone throughout its development.
attractions.hours: Tuesday – Saturday, 10:00 A.M. – 4:00 P.M.
attractions.admission: $5 General Admission; $4 Senior, Veteran, and Student Admissions; Free for Children 12 and Under; Free for SHM and ROAM Members
highlights: 4,000 square feet of indoor exhibit space with permanent and rotating galleries
Outdoor train exhibit featuring a Steam Locomotive, Caboose, and Pullman Business Car
Historic one-room Glendale Schoolhouse
Located in a Nationally Registered Historic Building since 1932
Research Center with reference library and thousands of artifacts
Free parking available
attractions.tips: Train tours are available by calling to schedule in advance. The museum is located in Victorian Square across from the Nugget Casino Resort. Admission is free on Thursday evenings from 4-7 PM during the nearby farmers market. Plan for 1-2 hours to fully explore the museum.
attractions.nearby: Nugget Casino Resort, Last Chance Joe, Victorian Square shops and restaurants, Sparks Marina, Downtown Sparks farmers market

attractions.name: Sparks Marina Park
attractions.description: Sparks Marina Park is an 81-acre recreational park centered around the 77-acre Helms Lake in Sparks, Nevada. The park offers a wide variety of activities including swimming, boating, fishing, and a 2-mile paved perimeter trail. It also features picnic areas, a playground, concession stand, Veterans Memorial, and a dog park, making it a popular destination for families and outdoor enthusiasts in the Truckee Meadows region.
attractions.hours: 6:00 AM - 10:00 PM daily (per City of Sparks); Tripadvisor lists 7:00 AM - 9:00 PM. Hours may vary seasonally.
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 77-acre Helms Lake for boating and fishing
2-mile paved perimeter walking/running trail
Designated swimming area with lifeguard availability
Veterans Memorial and playground
Dog park area
Picnic areas with restrooms and concession stand
attractions.tips: The Goodies Cafe concession stand is typically only open on weekends during the school year and daily in summer. Motorized boats with electric motors are allowed. Swimming is only permitted at the designated north beach area when lifeguards are on duty. Some visitors have reported safety concerns near the Legends Bay Casino side of the marina in the evening. The loop around the lake is approximately 1.9-2 miles.
attractions.nearby: Outlets at Legends Sparks, Legends Bay Casino, Atlantis Casino, National Automobile Museum, Greater Nevada Field, Wild Island, Truckee River Walk, Peppermill Casino, Seven Troughs Distilling Co

attractions.name: Victorian Square
attractions.description: Victorian Square is a revitalized downtown plaza and historic district in Sparks, Nevada, centered around the old railroad switchyard. The area blends old-fashioned architectural aesthetics with modern attractions including restaurants, bars with live music, a movie theater, and public art installations. It serves as a vibrant hub for community events, dining, arts, and entertainment, and is home to the Sparks Museum and Cultural Center and the iconic Last Chance Joe statue.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours (public plaza/neighborhood district)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Historic downtown plaza centered on Sparks' railroad heritage
Home to the Sparks Museum and Cultural Center with train exhibits and 1864 schoolhouse
Features Last Chance Joe, a 36-foot statue of the Nugget Casino's former mascot
Sparks Art Walk with rotating large-scale public art installations by local and regional artists
Dining and entertainment including Great Basin Brewery (Nevada's oldest operating brewery), restaurants, bars with live music, and a movie theater
Hosts major events like Hot August Nights car show and Hometowne Christmas Celebration
attractions.tips: Visit on the third Thursday of each month for the guided Sparks Art Walk tour. The Sparks Museum and Cultural Center is open Tuesday through Friday 11 AM to 4 PM, and Saturday 1 PM to 4 PM (closed Sundays and Mondays) — plan accordingly if you want to visit the museum. Parking is generally free outside daytime hours. Victorian Square is very walkable and pairs well with a visit to the nearby Nugget Casino Resort.
attractions.nearby: Nugget Casino Resort, Sparks Museum and Cultural Center, Sparks Marina Park, Great Basin Brewery, Revision Brewing Company, Reno Arch (Reno), Nevada Museum of Art (Reno)

attractions.name: Wild Island Family Adventure Park
attractions.description: Wild Island Family Adventure Park is Northern Nevada's premier family entertainment destination, featuring a waterpark, bowling, miniature golf, go-karts, laser tag, arcade, and XD adventure theater. Located in Sparks, it offers year-round attractions at Coconut Bowl and seasonal waterpark fun from May through September. The park caters to all ages with both indoor and outdoor activities.
attractions.hours: Coconut Bowl (Year-Round): 10:00am - 12:00am (Mon-Thu, Sun); 10:00am - 12:00am (Fri-Sat). Waterpark (Seasonal, May-Sept): 11:00am - 7:00pm daily (weather permitting). Individual attractions: Arcade 10am-12am, Bowling 10am-12am, Go Karts 10am-12am, Indoor Playground 10am-12am, Laser Tag 10am-12am, Mini Golf 10am-12am, Paradise Pro Shop 10am-4pm, Water Dogs Bar & Grill 10am-11:30pm, XD Interactive Theater 10am-12am
attractions.admission: Waterpark: General Admission $42.99 (48"+), Junior Admission $36.99 (under 48"), Senior $15.99 (60+), Children 3 & Under $5.00, After 3pm $34.99, Military $32.99. Season Pass $159.95, Military Season Pass $139.95, Senior Season Pass $79.95. All-Access Wristband $57.99. 3 Pack Attraction Pass $37.99. Waterpark Value Days $29.95. Children 3 & Under free for non-waterpark attractions.
highlights: Northern Nevada's only waterpark with slides, wave pool, lazy river, and Tiny Tides kids zone
36 holes of themed miniature golf (outdoor and blacklight/glow)
Coconut Bowl with bowling, arcade, and redemption games
Jungle Karts go-kart track and Elite Force Laser Tag
XD Adventure Theater immersive motion experience
High Ballocity indoor playground and Jambo Glow Golf
attractions.tips: The waterpark is seasonal and closed from October through April (reopens May 2026). Pack sunscreen and towels for waterpark visits. Bags are searched at the waterpark entrance, so don't try to sneak in outside food. Season passes are worth it if visiting more than a few times. Seniors, military, and children 3 & under receive discounts. Booking ahead online is recommended, especially for waterpark visits.
attractions.nearby: Legends at Sparks Marina, Bass Pro Shops Outdoor World, Sparks Heritage Museum, Nugget Casino Resort, Reno-Sparks Convention Center

attractions.name: Carson City Historic District (West Side Historic District)
attractions.description: The Carson City Historic District, also known as the West Side Historic District, was created in 1982 by the Carson City Board of Supervisors and encompasses the largest historical home district in Nevada. The district features nearly 50 landmarks including 1800s-era Victorian-style homes, museums, churches, and government buildings, all connected by the famous Kit Carson Trail (formerly the Blue Line Trail). Development within the district is overseen by the Historic Resources Commission to preserve its historic character and architectural heritage.
attractions.address: Bounded roughly by Curry, Mountain, 5th, and John Streets
attractions.hours: Open year-round, best enjoyed sunrise to sunset
attractions.admission: Free (self-guided walking tour); individual attractions within the district (museums, etc.) may have separate admission fees
highlights: Kit Carson Trail - 2.5-mile self-guided walking/driving tour with nearly 50 historic landmarks
Nevada State Museum - former U.S. Mint building housing rock, mining, and prehistoric exhibits
Nevada Governor's Mansion - historic mansion in the heart of the district
Victorian-era homes including the Krebs-Peterson House (John Wayne's last movie location) and Sears-Ferris House (boyhood home of the Ferris Wheel inventor)
Orion Clemens House - where Mark Twain (Samuel Clemens) stayed during his time in Nevada
Brewery Arts Center - housed in the former Carson Brewing Company building
attractions.tips: Pick up a free Kit Carson Trail map at the Visit Carson City Visitor Center at 716 N. Carson Street. The trail can be walked, biked, or driven. Visit early morning for best light and fewer crowds. Consider the seasonal Carson City Ghost Walk for a spooky evening tour. Respect private residences along the trail. The trail passes through both residential and commercial areas with plenty of dining and shopping options along Carson Street.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Capitol, Nevada State Railroad Museum, Stewart Indian School Cultural Center & Museum, Carson River Park, Virginia City (30 minutes), Lake Tahoe (30 minutes), Reno (30 minutes)

attractions.name: Jack C. Davis Observatory
attractions.description: The Jack C. Davis Observatory is an astronomical observatory located at Western Nevada College in Carson City, Nevada. The facility features three telescopes including two 0.4 m reflecting telescopes and one 0.25 m reflector, along with a 0.12 m robotic refractor for sunspot studies. It is open to the public for free Saturday night star parties with lectures and guided stargazing, and serves as a research-level facility for WNC students and the community.
attractions.hours: Saturday 6:30 PM - 10:00 PM (doors open at 6:30 PM between October 1 and March 31; 7:00 PM between April 1 and September 30). Lectures start at 7:00 PM (Oct-Mar) or 7:30 PM (Apr-Sep).
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Three telescopes: two 0.4 m reflecting telescopes and one 0.25 m reflector, plus a 0.12 m robotic refractor
Free weekly Saturday night star parties with lectures and guided stargazing
Planetary Walkway connecting the main campus to the observatory with sandstone planet monuments
Campbell Scientific research-grade weather station for solar-powered data collection
Affiliated with the Western Nevada Astronomical Society (WNAS)
Built by inmates from the Nevada State Prison at the prison's old quarry
attractions.tips: Star parties are held only on Saturday nights, so plan your visit accordingly. Arrive early when doors open (6:30 PM in winter, 7:00 PM in summer) to get good parking and settle in before the lecture. Bring warm clothing as it can get cold at night on the hillside. The observatory is weather-dependent, so check ahead on cloudy nights. You can drive or hike up to the observatory from the college campus.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Museum, Nevada State Railroad Museum, Nevada State Capitol Building, Capitol Complex, Governor's Mansion, Children's Museum of Northern Nevada, Casino Fandango, Charlie B Gallery, Carson Nugget

attractions.name: Kings Canyon Waterfall
attractions.description: Kings Canyon Waterfall is a scenic 25-foot cascading waterfall located just a 5-minute drive west of downtown Carson City. The short 0.25-mile hike to the falls makes this one of the most accessible waterfall hikes near Carson City, with options to extend to a 3-mile loop trail. The waterfall is most impressive in spring during snowmelt but remains a peaceful destination year-round. The trailhead is operated in partnership with the US Forest Service and Carson City Parks, Recreation, and Open Space.
attractions.address: 4450 Kings Canyon Rd, Carson City, NV 89703
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week (day use only; no overnight camping permitted). Weather-related seasonal closures may occur.
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 25-foot cascading waterfall just 5 minutes from downtown Carson City
Short 0.25-mile hike to the falls with minimal technical difficulty
Option to extend to a 3-mile loop trail through the Kings Canyon Trail System
Protected by US Forest Service with newly renovated trailhead, paved parking, and restrooms
Best flow during spring snowmelt for peak waterfall viewing
Wildlife viewing opportunities including lizards, snakes, and black bear/mountain lion habitat
attractions.tips: Visit in spring (March-May) when snowmelt provides the strongest waterfall flow. Wear sturdy footwear as the trail can be muddy and wet near the falls. Dogs are not permitted on the Waterfall Trail or North Kings Loop to protect Carson City's drinking water supply. Arrive early on weekends as parking can be limited during peak seasons. Bring water and snacks — no amenities are available at the trailhead. Be aware this is black bear and mountain lion habitat. The trail has no shade until you reach the falls, so it can get hot in summer afternoons.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Capitol Building, Nevada State Museum, Nevada State Railroad Museum, Kit Carson Trail, Washoe Lake State Park, Governor's Mansion, Capitol Complex, Carson City Hot Springs, Prison Hill Recreation Area

attractions.name: Nevada Governor's Mansion
attractions.description: The Nevada Governor's Mansion is the official residence of the governor of Nevada and has served in that capacity since 1909. Designed by Reno architect George A. Ferris in the Classical Revival (Neoclassical) style with Georgian and Jeffersonian motifs, the mansion is listed on the U.S. National Register of Historic Places. It is located in the heart of Carson City's historic district and offers exterior viewing year-round, with interior tours available on Nevada Day and select special events.
attractions.hours: Exterior viewing available anytime. Interior tours by special event only (e.g., Nevada Day parade day 2–4 PM). No regular public hours as it is a private residence.
attractions.days_open: Exterior viewable daily; interior tours by special event only
attractions.admission: Free (exterior viewing only; interior tours by special event)
highlights: Classical Revival (Neoclassical) architecture with Georgian and Jeffersonian motifs, designed by Reno architect George A. Ferris
Listed on the U.S. National Register of Historic Places (added 1976)
Official residence of Nevada governors since 1909, first occupied by Acting Governor Denver S. Dickerson
Part of the Kit Carson Trail historic walking tour
Known as one of Nevada's most haunted places, featured on Ghost Walk tours (June–October)
Two-story pediment portico with fluted Ionic columns and wraparound second-story porch
attractions.tips: The mansion is a private residence, so interior tours are not regularly available — exterior viewing and photos are welcome. The best times to see it up close are during the Nevada Day parade (October) when interior tours are offered, or during the Carson City Ghost Walk tours (June–October). It is located about 4 blocks from the Nevada State Capitol and is a stop on the Kit Carson Trail walking tour — follow the blue line on the sidewalk through Carson City's historic district.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Museum (0.3 mi), Nevada State Capitol Building (0.5 mi), Carson Nugget Casino (0.4 mi), Capitol Complex (0.5 mi), Stewart Indian Cultural Center, Charlie B Gallery, Clear Creek Trail

attractions.name: Nevada State Capitol Building
attractions.description: The Nevada State Capitol is the capitol building of the U.S. state of Nevada, located in Carson City at 101 North Carson Street. Built between 1869 and 1871 in the Neoclassical Italianate style, it is the second oldest capitol building west of the Mississippi River. Though the Legislature and Supreme Court have since moved to adjacent buildings, the Capitol still houses the offices of the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, and Secretary of State. The building features a museum on its second floor (Battle Born Hall) and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
attractions.hours: Monday–Friday: 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM; Saturday: 9:00 AM – 3:00 PM; Sunday: Closed. Battle Born Hall (museum): Monday–Friday 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Saturday 9:00 AM – 3:00 PM, Sunday Closed. Saturday guided tours at the top of every hour from 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM (walk-in, first-come first-serve).
attractions.admission: Free — Admission to Battle Born Hall, self-guided tours, and docent-led tours of the Nevada State Capitol are all free to the public.
highlights: Second oldest capitol building west of the Mississippi River
Neoclassical Italianate architecture built 1869–1871
Battle Born Hall museum on the second floor with Nevada history exhibits
Still houses the offices of the Governor, Lieutenant Governor, and Secretary of State
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places
Set on the beautiful Nevada State Capitol Mall grounds
attractions.tips: Pick up the Historical Capitol Building Walking Tour brochure for a self-guided tour. For guided tours on weekdays, call at least two weeks in advance as they are subject to volunteer docent availability. Saturday guided tours are walk-in only at the top of every hour from 10 AM to 3 PM. The building is closed on Sundays and state holidays. Free street parking is available nearby.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Museum (600 N Carson St), Nevada State Railroad Museum, Kit Carson Trail, Carson City Historic District, Stewart Indian School Cultural Center, Nevada State Legislature Building, Carson City Civic Center

attractions.name: Nevada State Museum, Carson City
attractions.description: The Nevada State Museum in Carson City is one of seven Nevada State Museums operated by the Nevada Department of Tourism and Cultural Affairs. Housed in the former Carson City Mint building (1870–1893), the museum features exhibits on Nevada's natural and cultural heritage from the prehistoric period to the present. Key displays include the world's largest exhibited Columbian mammoth, the silver service from the USS Nevada, and exhibits on geology, silver mining, and American Indian culture.
attractions.hours: Tuesday through Sunday: 8:30 am to 4:30 pm
attractions.admission: Free for Museum Members; $10 Adults; Free for Children 17 & Under
highlights: World's largest exhibited Columbian mammoth, found in the Black Rock Desert
Historic Carson City Mint with Coin Press No. 1 and CC-marked Comstock silver coins
Silver service from the USS Nevada battleship, lined with gold from Goldfield
Fey Slot Machine Collection and guns of the Old West
Nevada: A People and Place through Time exhibit covering state history
Under One Sky exhibit on Great Basin Native American history spanning 10,000 years
attractions.tips: The museum is closed Mondays, so plan your visit for Tuesday through Sunday. Arrive early to allow enough time to explore all exhibits, as the museum closes at 4:30 pm. Museum members receive free admission, so consider becoming a member if you plan multiple visits. The historic mint building itself is an architectural attraction worth seeing.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Capitol Building, Carson City State Railroad Museum, Nevada State Printing Office, Governor's Mansion, Virginia City (historic mining town nearby)

attractions.name: Nevada State Railroad Museum, Carson City
attractions.description: The Nevada State Railroad Museum in Carson City preserves the history of Nevada's railroads with an extensive collection of historic locomotives, passenger cars, and railroad equipment. The museum features both indoor and outdoor exhibits including a roundhouse, turntable, and operational steam and diesel locomotives. Train rides are offered on select weekends, holidays, and special events, providing visitors with an immersive railroading experience.
attractions.hours: Thursday through Monday: 9:00 AM – 4:30 PM (Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays)
attractions.admission: Adults: $10.00; Children 17 & under: Free; Museum Members: Free. Train rides are not included in museum admission and are offered on select weekends, holidays, and special events.
highlights: Historic steam and diesel locomotives on display
Operational train rides on select weekends and holidays
Roundhouse and turntable exhibits
Vintage railroad passenger cars and rolling stock
Museum store with railroad-themed gifts and memorabilia
Special events including Harvest Train Festival and Eggstravaganza
attractions.tips: The museum is closed on Tuesdays and Wednesdays, so plan your visit accordingly. Train rides are not included in general admission — check the website for the current train ride schedule before visiting. The museum is closed on Thanksgiving, Christmas Day, and New Year's Day. Parking is available on-site. Allow 1-2 hours to explore the museum and grounds.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Museum (Carson City), Capitol Building, Nevada State Capitol Grounds, Virginia City (historic mining town, ~20 miles), Lake Tahoe (~30 miles)

attractions.name: Stewart Indian School Cultural Center & Museum
attractions.description: The Stewart Indian School Cultural Center & Museum is dedicated to preserving and sharing the stories of the children who attended the Stewart Indian School, a federal boarding school that operated from 1890 to 1980. The museum serves as a steward of living American Indian materials and traditions, honoring the Great Basin tribes whose homelands this land once was. It features exhibits, a Great Basin Native Artists Gallery, and a historic walking trail through the former school campus.
attractions.hours: Monday through Friday, 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Historic Stewart Indian School campus with preserved buildings from the federal boarding school era (1890-1980)
Great Basin Native Artists Gallery showcasing contemporary Indigenous art
Walking Trail through the historic school grounds
Museum exhibits telling the stories of students and their families
Annual Stewart Indian School Reunion event
Dedicated to the memories of Great Basin tribes including Numu (Northern Paiute), Nuwu (Southern Paiute), and Wašiw (Washoe)
attractions.tips: Admission is free but RSVPs are appreciated for special events like the annual reunion. The museum is closed on weekends, so plan your visit for Monday through Friday. Contact the museum ahead of time to confirm hours for group visits. Explore the walking trail to see the full historic campus grounds.
attractions.nearby: Nevada State Museum (Carson City), Nevada State Capitol Building, Carson City Historic District, Brewery Arts Center, Lake Tahoe (approximately 30 minutes away)

attractions.name: Washoe Lake State Park
attractions.description: Nestled in a scenic valley where bald eagles soar, Washoe Lake State Park offers breathtaking views of the Sierra Nevada, Carson and Virginia mountain ranges. A popular area for hiking, camping, picnicking, water sports and equestrian activities, the park also hosts a diverse range of birds and waterfowl. Anglers can aim for channel and bullhead catfish, wipers, white bass and Sacramento Perch. Situated between Reno and Carson City, the park is a true treasure for local residents and visitors to enjoy year-round.
attractions.hours: Always Open (24 hours)
attractions.days_open: Open seven days a week, 365 days a year
attractions.admission: Day use entrance fee: $5.00 per vehicle (Non-NV Vehicles $10.00 per vehicle). Boat launch: $10.00 (Non-NV Vehicles: $15.00). Camping: $15.00 per vehicle, per night (Non-NV Vehicles: $20.00 per vehicle, per night) + $10.00 for sites with utilities. Camp and boat: $20.00 per night (Non-NV Vehicles: $25.00 per night) + $10.00 for sites with utilities. Bike in: $2.00 per bike.
highlights: Breathtaking views of the Sierra Nevada, Carson, and Virginia mountain ranges
18-hole disc golf course with natural obstacles including Washoe Lake itself
49 campsites with tables, grills, fire rings, some with shade structures and utility hookups
Boat launching ramps at main day use area, North Ramp, and Little Washoe
Diverse birdwatching and wildlife viewing, including bald eagles and American white pelicans
Equestrian facilities with arena, corrals, horse washing station, and group camping for up to 200
attractions.tips: Driving on the beach is prohibited — vehicles must stay on designated roads. No drones or remote-controlled aircraft allowed. Pets must be on a leash no longer than six feet in camping or picnic areas. Bring insect repellent, closed-toed shoes, and high socks for the disc golf course due to ticks, mosquitoes, and midges. Do not feed or harass the feral horses. Respect Leave No Trace and Tread Lightly principles.
attractions.nearby: Mormon Station State Park (Genoa), Fort Churchill State Historic Park (Silver Springs), Spooner Lake & Backcountry (Incline Village), Lake Tahoe, Carson City

attractions.name: Comstock Gold Mill
attractions.description: The Comstock Gold Mill is Virginia City's last fully operational stamp mill, dating back to the 1860s and built by Joshua Hendy in San Francisco as a two-stamp gold mill. Visitors can see how gold was processed from ore to recovery through a narrated, educational tour featuring extensive historical mining equipment. This enriching cultural center helps preserve the mining heritage of the Comstock Lode.
attractions.hours: Thursday through Monday: 11:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m.
attractions.days_open: Seasonal: May through October, Thursday through Monday
attractions.admission: Adults: $10; Children ages 12-18: $5; Children 12 and under: Free. Donations are encouraged.
highlights: Last fully operational stamp mill from the 1860s in Virginia City
Built by Joshua Hendy in San Francisco as a two-stamp gold mill
Narrated educational tour showing gold ore processing and recovery
Extensive collection of historical mining equipment in one location
Located on F Street near the V&T railroad tracks
Large groups up to 60 accommodated by appointment
attractions.tips: The mill is open seasonally (May-October) only Thursday through Monday from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m., so plan your visit accordingly. Large groups should call ahead to make reservations and ask about school/tour group discounts. The mill depends on donations beyond admission fees, so consider contributing extra to support preservation. The mill is located near the old amphitheater where V&T railroad tracks cross F Street.
attractions.nearby: Comstock History Center, Historic Fourth Ward School Museum & Archives, Virginia & Truckee Railroad, Ponderosa Mine Tour, Mackay Mansion, Way It Was Museum, Chollar Mine Tours, St. Mary's in the Mountains Church

attractions.name: Comstock History Center
attractions.description: The Comstock History Center is a museum and cultural center located just a few blocks from Virginia City's Historic C Street, showcasing a unique collection of Comstock artifacts, photos, and memorabilia. It proudly houses the original #27 Virginia & Truckee Railroad steam locomotive and serves as the office for the Comstock Historic District Commission. The center celebrates Virginia City's role as the site of the United States' largest silver strike and the richest city in the West.
attractions.address: 20 North E Street, Virginia City, NV 89440
attractions.hours: Museum: Wednesday 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Thursday 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Friday 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Saturday 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM. Offices (Comstock Historic District Commission): Monday – Friday 8:30 AM – 5:00 PM.
attractions.admission: Free (donations accepted)
highlights: Original #27 Virginia & Truckee Railroad steam locomotive on display
Unique collection of Comstock mining artifacts, photos, and memorabilia
Houses the Comstock Historic District Commission
Home to the Comstock Cemetery Foundation
Free admission with donations accepted
Located just two blocks from Historic C Street
attractions.tips: Admission is free but donations are appreciated to support exhibits and preservation. The museum is small and can be explored in under an hour. Visit the Virginia City Visitors Center on C Street for discounted museum passes and the Comstock Adventure Pass. The center is open only Wednesday through Saturday, so plan your visit accordingly. The museum is operated by passionate volunteers and is part of the Nevada State Museums system.
attractions.nearby: The Way It Was Museum, Storey County Courthouse & Slammer Museum, Historic Fourth Ward School Museum, Mackay Mansion, Piper's Opera House, Liberty Engine Company No. 1 Comstock Firemen's Museum, Silver State National Peace Officers Museum, Marshall Mint & Museum, Virginia City Visitor Center, Silver Terrace Cemetery, Red Dog Saloon, Comstock Lode

attractions.name: Historic Fourth Ward School Museum
attractions.description: The Historic Fourth Ward School Museum is an iconic 4-story mansard-roofed former public school building located in Virginia City, Nevada. Designed in 1876 by architect C. M. Bennett in the Second Empire style, it was built to accommodate over 1,000 students and is the last one standing in the U.S. of this design and architecture built of wood. Reopened as a museum in 1986, it features exhibits including the Vintage Classroom, Comstock Lode history, Mark Twain exhibits, and modern mining and minerals. It is a contributing property in the Virginia City Historic District, which is a National Historic Landmark.
attractions.hours: Regular Season (May 1 - October 31): Daily 10:00 AM - 5:00 PM. Winter Season (November 1 - April 28): Fridays, Saturdays, Sundays, and Mondays 11:00 AM - 3:00 PM (closed Tuesdays through Thursdays). Winter hours are weather permitting.
attractions.days_open: Daily (Regular Season); Fridays through Mondays (Winter Season)
attractions.admission: Adults (Age 13+): $9.00 | Children (Age 6-12): $6.00 | Children (Age 5 and under): Free | Family Pass (2 Adults + 2 Children): $25 | Museum Members: Free | Blue Star Museum: Free for active military and immediate family with current Military ID | Online advance purchase saves $1 per ticket
highlights: Vintage Classroom exhibit recreating a 19th-century schoolroom
Comstock Lode history exhibits
Mark Twain life and printing exhibits
Modern Mining and Minerals gallery
Last standing Second Empire-style wooden schoolhouse in the United States
Alumni and changing gallery exhibits
attractions.tips: Call ahead during winter months as hours are weather permitting and the museum may close due to snow. Purchase tickets online in advance to save $1 per ticket. Family passes ($25) offer better value for groups with children. Only pencils are allowed in the galleries. Flash photography is permitted for personal use. The museum is currently open for regular season (April 18 is before May 1, so winter hours apply — call ahead to confirm).
attractions.nearby: Piper's Opera House, Virginia City Historic District, Comstock Gold Mill, St. Mary's in the Mountains Catholic Church, Nevada State Railroad Museum - Virginia City, Silver Queen Hotel, Old Washoe Club

attractions.name: International Camel & Ostrich Races
attractions.description: The International Camel & Ostrich Races is a historic annual event held in Virginia City, Nevada, where camels, ostriches, zebras, emus, and more race around a track with professional and amateur jockeys. Now in its 67th year, this Nevada tradition began in 1959 as the result of a prank war between the Territorial Enterprise and the San Francisco Chronicle. The three-day festival features multiple shows, fan contests, food trucks, craft vendors, and even the opportunity for visitors to race a camel themselves. It's one of the must-see events in the West and typically sells out quickly.
attractions.hours: Event held annually in September (typically the first or second weekend). Gates open 2 hours prior to each show. Friday: 5:00 PM. Saturday: 10:00 AM and 2:00 PM. Sunday: 12:00 PM (Family Day). For 2026: September 11-13.
attractions.days_open: Annual event held for 3 days (Friday, Saturday, Sunday) in September
attractions.admission: General Admission: $14-$20 (adults, kids & seniors). Premium Shaded Bleacher Seating: $30. VIP Bleacher Seating / VIP Interior Table / VIP Front Row Table: $65-$450. Race a Camel (includes 2 VIP seats, lunch & drink coupons): $500. VIP Parking: $35. Kids 2 and under: Free (must sit on lap). First 100 kids on Family Day: Free camel ride.
highlights: Professional and amateur jockeys race camels, ostriches, zebras, and emus
Hot Camel Nights Friday evening show with discounted local rates
Family Day Sunday with free camel rides for first 100 kids
Opportunity for visitors to race a camel themselves ($500 package)
Food trucks, craft vendors, event bars, and official merchandise
One of Nevada's oldest and most iconic annual traditions since 1959
attractions.tips: Arrive early (gates open 2 hours before showtime) to find good seats and enjoy the pre-show atmosphere. Bring sunscreen, a hat, and sunglasses as the fairgrounds are largely unshaded outside of premium seating. No umbrellas, pets (except service animals), outside food/drink, or weapons allowed. Consider the VIP package for shaded seating, air-conditioned tent, lunch, and drink tickets. Parking is available at the fairgrounds and in town with a complimentary golf cart shuttle to the arena. Event is rain or shine and tickets are non-refundable.
attractions.nearby: Virginia City Historic District, Piper's Opera House, Way It Was Museum, Virginia & Truckee Railroad, St. Mary in the Mountains Catholic Church, Comstock Mining history sites, Nevada State Railroad Museum Carson City

attractions.name: Liberty Engine Company #1 Comstock Firemen's Museum
attractions.description: Founded in 1979 by Virginia City's volunteer Fire Department, the Liberty Engine Company #1 Comstock Firemen's Museum displays a remarkable collection of 19th-century firefighting equipment and artifacts from the Comstock mining era. The museum features restored fire pumpers, horse-drawn fire vehicles, helmets, uniforms, and glass fire grenades. Liberty Engine Company No. 1 remains an operating volunteer firefighting unit to this day.
attractions.hours: Monday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Tuesday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Wednesday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Saturday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM, Sunday: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM (Seasonal: May through October)
attractions.admission: Free (donations appreciated)
highlights: Restored 19th-century horse-drawn fire pumpers and ladder wagons
Collection of vintage firefighter helmets, uniforms, and belts
Glass fire grenades and other unique firefighting artifacts
Operating volunteer firefighting unit still based at the museum
Extensive Nevada and Comstock mining area firefighting history exhibits
Historic fire station building on Virginia City's main street
attractions.tips: While admission is free, please consider making a donation to support the museum's preservation efforts. Plan about 30-60 minutes for your visit.
