The museum is small but packed with fascinating artifacts. It's only open seasonally from May through October, so verify hours before visiting. Dogs are allowed.
attractions.nearby: The Washoe Club Haunted Museum, Historic Fourth Ward School Museum, The Way It Was Museum, Silver State National Peace Officers Museum, Marshall Mint & Museum, Piper's Opera House, Virginia City Visitor Center, Comstock History Center, St. Mary in the Mountains Catholic Church

attractions.name: Mackay Mansion
attractions.description: The Mackay Mansion is one of the oldest and best-preserved buildings in Virginia City, Nevada, built in 1859 by George Hearst as the headquarters of the Gould & Curry Mining Company. Later purchased by silver king John Mackay, the mansion features original Victorian furnishings, Tiffany silver, chandeliers, and mining artifacts. It is also known as one of Nevada's most haunted locations, having been featured on Ghost Adventures and The Dead Files.
attractions.hours: Summer: Friday-Saturday 10:00 AM – 6:00 PM, Sunday-Thursday 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM. Winter: Sunday-Saturday 12:00 PM – 5:00 PM (weather permitting). Hours subject to change.
attractions.admission: Day Tour: Adults $14, Kids 6-12 $7, Under 5 Free. After Hours Tour (5-6 PM): $25 per person (reservation required, minimum 4 people). Comstock Adventure Pass also accepted.
highlights: Built in 1859 by George Hearst, one of the oldest buildings in Virginia City
Original Victorian-era furnishings, carpet, wallpaper, and chandeliers still intact
Featured on Ghost Adventures and The Dead Files paranormal TV shows
Historic vault where gold bullion bars were sold from 1860 to 1950
Guided day tours and after-hours paranormal night investigations available
Available for weddings, private events, and ghost hunting expeditions
attractions.tips: Tours run approximately 30 minutes and leave on a first-come, first-served basis. If a tour is in progress when you arrive, the door may be locked — wait on the porch and the guide will get you. The last tour departs 30 minutes before closing. After-hours paranormal tours require advance reservations. Cash is preferred for payment. Tours may be weather-dependent in winter.
attractions.nearby: Piper's Opera House, St. Mary's Art Center, Storey County Courthouse, Comstock Gold Museum, Gould & Curry Mill ruins, Virginia City Historic District, Territorial Enterprise Building, Old Washoe Club

attractions.name: Piper's Opera House
attractions.description: Piper's Opera House is an historic performing arts venue in Virginia City, Nevada, built in 1885 after two previous structures burned down. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places and the League of Historic Theaters, it hosted legendary performers including Mark Twain, Al Jolson, Lilly Langtry, and Edwin Booth. Today it operates as a fully-functioning performing arts center offering tours, theatrical performances, concerts, and private events.
attractions.address: 12 North B Street, PO Box Drawer J, Virginia City, NV 89440
attractions.hours: Monday: Closed; Tuesday: Closed; Wednesday: 12:00 PM - 4:00 PM; Thursday: 12:00 PM - 4:00 PM; Friday: 12:00 PM - 4:00 PM; Saturday: 12:00 PM - 4:00 PM; Sunday: 12:00 PM - 4:00 PM. Tours offered April through October. Corner Bar hours: Friday-Sunday 11:00 AM - 12:00 AM; Monday, Tuesday, Thursday 11:00 AM - 9:00 PM; Closed Wednesdays.
attractions.admission: Self-guided tours: $3 per person (Travel Nevada) / $10 adults, $5 children (official website). Performance ticket prices vary by event.
highlights: Historic 1885 building listed on the National Register of Historic Places and League of Historic Theaters
Hosted legendary performers including Mark Twain, Al Jolson, Lilly Langtry, and Edwin Booth
Self-guided tours revealing original architecture, historical photos, and period furnishings
The Corner Bar — one of the longest continually operating saloons in Virginia City
Fully-functioning performing arts center with live theatrical performances and concerts
Rumored to be haunted by spirits of past performers and patrons
attractions.tips: Tours are only offered April through October, so plan your visit during those months. Tickets can be purchased at Piper's Opera House or the Virginia City Visitor Center on C Street. The building is accessible via the main doors with an elevator to the main floor. Check the events calendar before visiting as the venue hosts performances that may affect tour availability. Consider stopping at the Corner Bar for a drink — it's been operating since the 1860s.
attractions.nearby: Virginia City Visitor Center, Comstock History Center, Nevada State Railroad Museum, B Street House Bed and Breakfast, Comstock Brewery, St. Mary's in the Mountains Catholic Church, Virginia City Mining Museum, Way It Was Museum, Fourth Ward School Museum, Virginia City Cemetery

attractions.name: Silver Queen Hotel
attractions.description: The Silver Queen Hotel is a historic hotel located in the center of Virginia City, Nevada. Built in 1876, it is the oldest hotel in Virginia City and features 28 restored rooms dating back to the 19th century. The hotel is famous for its "Silver Queen" painting—a 15-foot full-body portrait of a woman embedded with 3,261 Morgan silver dollars—and also houses an Old West Saloon and a historic wedding chapel.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours for guests; saloon hours vary by season
attractions.admission: Room rates starting at $109-$185 per night depending on room type
highlights: 28 restored 19th-century guest rooms with period furnishings
Famous Silver Queen painting embedded with 3,261 Morgan silver dollars
Historic Old West Saloon with single-piece wooden back bars from the 1870s
On-site historic wedding chapel where Captain and Tennille were married
Oldest hotel in Virginia City, built in 1876
Maintains authentic historical ambiance without modern amenities like elevators or AC
attractions.tips: The hotel intentionally does not offer modern amenities like air conditioning, elevators, televisions, or Wi-Fi to preserve its historical character. Book in advance during peak tourist season. The saloon is a great place to experience Old West atmosphere. The wedding chapel is a popular spot for ceremonies.
attractions.nearby: Piper's Opera House, Virginia City Visitor Center, Washoe Club Museum & Saloon, Palace Restaurant & Saloon, St. Mary in the Mountains Church, Spanish Ravine

attractions.name: St. Mary's Art Center
attractions.description: Built in 1875 as St. Mary Louise Hospital and converted into an art center in 1964, St. Mary's Art Center is the Grand Dame of Virginia City and a cornerstone of the Comstock's arts community. The 501(c)(3) nonprofit features seven art galleries across four floors, a print room, art studio, library, theatre, and 16 guest rooms. It hosts art exhibitions, artist retreats, classes, and special events while preserving the historic 1876 building.
attractions.hours: Gallery & Tour Open Days: by private appointment, as available. Tours are offered Tuesdays through Saturdays by appointment.
attractions.admission: $6 for 45-minute tour; donations encouraged. Seasonal open days may have free admission — check calendar for details.
highlights: Historic 1875 building — formerly St. Mary Louise Hospital, now celebrating its 150th anniversary
Seven art galleries across four floors featuring permanent and rotating exhibitions
16 guest rooms available for overnight stays and artist retreats
Art classes in acrylic, oil, pastel, watercolor, sculpture, and photography
Original hand-painted cork flooring from the hospital era
Print room, art studio, library, and theatre on-site
attractions.tips: Contact the center in advance to confirm open days and schedule a tour, as hours vary seasonally. The center is currently closed for season but opens April 8th for the 2026 season. Private tours and group rentals are available year-round. Book online at stmarysartcenter.org or call (775) 440-0992 for the director. Consider staying overnight in one of the 16 guest rooms for a full retreat experience.
attractions.nearby: Saint Mary in the Mountains Catholic Church, Comstock History Center, Virginia City historic downtown, Piper's Opera House, Way It Was Museum

attractions.name: The Way It Was Museum
attractions.description: The Way It Was Museum in historic Virginia City houses the most complete collection of Comstock memorabilia in the world. Visitors can explore rare photographs, mining and mineral artifacts from the Comstock Lode, vintage maps, lithographs, and original relics that tell the story of the richest city in the American West. The museum features a scale model of the hundreds of miles of underground mine shafts beneath Virginia City, historic photography, vintage costumes, and a Mark Twain exhibit.
attractions.address: 113 North C Street, Virginia City, NV 89440
attractions.hours: 10:30 AM – 4:30 PM (Daily)
attractions.days_open: Open daily, year round (closed Christmas Day per some sources)
attractions.admission: Adults: $7; Children 11 and under: Free
highlights: Most complete collection of Comstock memorabilia in the world
Scale model of hundreds of miles of underground mine shafts beneath Virginia City
Rare photographs, lithographs, and vintage maps from the Bonanza period
Historic mining tools, equipment, and mineral samples including assay furnace
Mark Twain exhibit featuring photos and documents from his time at the Territorial Enterprise
Vintage costumes and artifacts depicting 1800s daily life in a mining town
attractions.tips: The museum is small but packed with content — plan for 30-45 minutes. No restrooms inside the museum. Admission is very affordable at $7. Consider purchasing a Comstock Adventure Pass at the Virginia City Visitors Center for access to multiple attractions. The museum is located on C Street, ranked as one of the Top 10 Historic Main Streets in America, so explore the surrounding area.
attractions.nearby: Marshall Mint & Museum, Silver Terrace Cemetery, Virginia City Mall, Comstock History Center, Piper's Opera House, St. Mary in the Mountains Catholic Church, Silver State National Peace Officers Museum, Storey County Courthouse, The Washoe Club Haunted Museum, Historic Fourth Ward School Museum, Liberty Engine Company No. 1 Comstock Firemen's Museum

attractions.name: Virginia & Truckee Railroad
attractions.description: The Virginia & Truckee Railroad, known as the "Queen of the Short Lines," offers scenic 35-minute narrated train rides from Virginia City's historic 1870 depot to Gold Hill and back. Originally completed in 1869, this iconic railroad once hauled millions in gold and silver from the Comstock mines, and today visitors ride through the heart of the historic mining region aboard diesel or steam trains.
attractions.hours: Daily train departures at 10:30 AM, 11:20 AM, 12:30 PM, 1:20 PM, 2:10 PM, 3:00 PM, and 4:00 PM (Memorial Day through October). Each ride is a 35-minute round trip. Operates 7 days a week during season.
attractions.admission: Adults: $18.00 | Children Age 5–12: $10.00 | Children 4 and under: Free | All-day Pass: $50.00
highlights: Historic 1870 depot, the original Virginia City railroad station
35-minute narrated scenic train rides through the Comstock mining region
Seven daily departures with views of historic mines and wild horses
Themed event trains including Pumpkin Patch Trains, Halloween Train, and Candy Cane Express
Opportunity to disembark in Gold Hill and return on a later train
Steam and diesel train options depending on season
attractions.tips: Arrive at least 15-30 minutes before departure as boarding closes 15 minutes prior. The last train of the day does not allow disembarkation in Gold Hill. Wear layers as the open-air train cars can be cool even on warm days. Check the website for seasonal schedule changes — the railroad operates Memorial Day through October. Consider the all-day pass if you want to explore Gold Hill and return on a later train.
attractions.nearby: Comstock History Center, Chollar Mine Tours, Gold Hill Hotel & Saloon, Virginia City Historic District, Pipers Opera House, St. Mary's in the Mountains Church, Nevada State Railroad Museum (Carson City)

attractions.name: Virginia City Historic District, Nevada
attractions.description: The Virginia City Historic District, a National Historic Landmark, includes the 19th century mining towns of Virginia City, Gold Hill, Silver City, and Dayton, along with open lands containing cultural sites associated with mining activities. Between 1860 and 1880, these towns were the model for what would become the classic frontier mining boom town in the American West, producing nearly seven million tons of ore worth approximately $700 million. The district features multiple museums, annual festivals, railroad rides, mine tours, and historic walking tours.
attractions.hours: Visitor Center: Monday-Saturday 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM, Sunday 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM (year round). Individual attractions vary — many close seasonally October through spring.
attractions.admission: Free to the Historic District. Individual attractions vary: Comstock Adventure Pass available for multiple attractions; some museums $4-$7 (e.g., The Way It Was Museum $7 adults, free children 11 and under). Ages 4 and under free.
highlights: National Historic Landmark designation
Comstock Lode silver mining heritage with nearly $700 million in historical ore value
Multiple museums including Fourth Ward School Museum, Way It Was Museum, and Comstock History Center
Virginia & Truckee Railroad rides and historic mine tours
Historic C Street with preserved 19th-century buildings, saloons, and shops
Annual festivals and Wild West comedy shows (May-October)
attractions.tips: Many shops and attractions open late morning (11 AM-12 PM), so plan accordingly. Some attractions close seasonally from October through spring — check ahead. The Comstock Adventure Pass offers good value for visiting multiple attractions. Located at 6,200 feet elevation, dress in layers. Parking is available on C Street and side streets. Book trolley tours and mine tours in advance during peak season.
attractions.nearby: Reno (20 miles northwest), Carson City (20 miles southeast), Lake Tahoe (30 miles southwest), Pyramid Lake (40 miles northeast), historic Fourth Ward School & Museum, Comstock History Center, Way It Was Museum, Virginia & Truckee Railroad

attractions.name: Bijou Community Park
attractions.description: Bijou Community Park is South Lake Tahoe's only true community park. Constructed in 1992, the park offers diverse recreation opportunities to both residents and visitors alike, including a bike park, disc golf course, skateboard park, dog park, volleyball courts, and covered gazebos for picnics.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to sunset (open daily)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Bike park with BMX/bike course and ramps
One of the top disc golf courses in the country
Fenced dog park open to the public
Skateboard park
Historic railroad exhibit
Two covered gazebos for picnics and events
attractions.tips: Parking is available at Lake Tahoe Community College across the street. The park is great for families and offers something for all ages. Visit during daylight hours as the park closes at sunset. Bring your own frisbee for disc golf, and if you're biking, there's a bike repair station on-site.
attractions.nearby: Tahoe Trout Farm (0.7 mi), Van Sickle Bi-State Park (2.4 mi), The Gondola at Heavenly (2.4 mi), Tallac Historic Site, Lake Tahoe Community College (across the street)

attractions.name: Cave Rock - Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park
attractions.description: Cave Rock is a prominent volcanic rock formation on the shores of Lake Tahoe, sacred to the Washoe Tribe. Operated by Nevada State Parks, it features a boat ramp, small beach, and day-use area with picnic spots. The site offers stunning views of Lake Tahoe and is a popular spot for boating, fishing, and photography along Highway 50.
attractions.hours: Park: Sunrise to sunset daily. Boat Launch: May 25–September 4: 6 a.m.–8 p.m.; September 5–May 24: 6 a.m.–4 p.m. (Winter: Oct 1–April 30, 6 a.m.–4 p.m.)
attractions.admission: Day use entrance: $10 (NV residents) / $15 (out-of-state). Boat launch: $20 (NV) / $25 (out-of-state). Bike in: $2. Photo stop (15 min): $2.
highlights: Sacred volcanic rock formation significant to the Washoe Tribe
Boat ramp with access to Lake Tahoe's crystalline waters
Small beach and picnic areas for day use
Scenic views along Highway 50
Good fishing location with rocky shoals
Photo stop opportunity at iconic rock formation
attractions.tips: Reservations are required for vehicle entry before 10:30 AM from April 15–October 15. Parking is limited (~3-4 cars), so arrive early for sunset visits. Consider the $2 photo stop fee if you're just stopping briefly. Boat launch hours vary by season, so check tahoeboatinspections.com before visiting.
attractions.nearby: Zephyr Cove, Sand Harbor State Park, Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park (main area), Tahoe Rim Trail, Glenbrook Lodge

attractions.name: D. L. Bliss State Park
attractions.description: D. L. Bliss State Park is a California State Park on the west shore of Lake Tahoe, featuring some of the lake's most pristine beaches, including the popular Lester Beach. The park offers hiking trails such as the Rubicon Trail and Balancing Rock Nature Trail, camping facilities, and opportunities for swimming, scuba diving, kayaking, and paddleboarding. Named after pioneering lumberman D. L. Bliss, the park provides stunning views of Emerald Bay and the surrounding Sierra Nevada mountains.
attractions.hours: Day Use Hours: Sunrise to Sunset
attractions.days_open: Daily (seasonal closure during winter off-season for vehicular access; park remains open to pedestrians)
attractions.admission: Day Use Parking Fee (varies by vehicle type). Tahoe Regional Annual Pass: $75. Camping fees apply (reservations via ReserveCalifornia.com).
highlights: Lester Beach - one of the most popular beaches in the Lake Tahoe region
Balancing Rock Nature Trail - half-mile self-guided trail featuring a 130-ton precariously balanced granite rock
Rubicon Trail - scenic hiking trail wrapping around Emerald Bay with stunning lake views
Crystal clear Lake Tahoe waters ideal for swimming, scuba diving, kayaking, and paddleboarding
Camping with approximately 17 campsites
Scenic views of Emerald Bay and Fannette Island
attractions.tips: The park closes to vehicular traffic during the winter off-season; pedestrians are still welcome. Arrive early during summer as parking fills quickly. Chains or 4-wheel drive may be required in winter. Cell phone reception may be limited. Bring layered clothing as weather can change abruptly. Bear-proof coolers must be locked per IGBC standards or face up to a $1,000 fine. Dogs are allowed only in developed areas (campsites, paved roads/paths).
attractions.nearby: Emerald Bay State Park (including Vikingsholm), Sugar Pine Point State Park, Eagle Falls, and the Rubicon Trail extending to Emerald Bay

attractions.name: Donner Memorial State Park
attractions.description: Donner Memorial State Park is a legendary alpine park in the Sierra Nevada mountains near Truckee, California, situated on the north shore of Donner Lake. The park commemorates the ill-fated Donner Party of 1846 and features the Pioneer Monument dedicated to pioneers who crossed the Sierra. It offers year-round outdoor recreation including camping, hiking, fishing, kayaking, cross-country skiing, and snowshoeing, along with a visitor center museum featuring exhibits on the Emigrant Experience and Donner Party history.
attractions.hours: Day Use: Open Sunrise to Sunset, 7 days a week. Visitor Center: Open Daily 10:00 a.m. – 5:00 p.m. (Closed Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's Day)
attractions.days_open: Open 7 days a week, year-round (day use sunrise to sunset; visitor center closed Thanksgiving, Dec 25, and Jan 1)
attractions.admission: Day Use: $10 per vehicle. Bus Fee: $100/day. Camping fees vary by site (reservations required via ReserveCalifornia). Visitor Center exhibits and museum: Included with day use fee.
highlights: Pioneer Monument — bronze monument dedicated to Sierra-crossing pioneers, completed in 1918 by the Native Sons of the Golden West
Visitor Center Museum — exhibits on the Donner Party of 1846 and the Emigrant Experience
Donner Lake shoreline — over 2 miles of lake frontage for swimming, fishing, and kayaking
Over 8 miles of hiking and over-the-snow trails including the Lakeside Interpretive Trail
154 campsites with picnic tables, restrooms, and bear-resistant food lockers
Year-round activities — summer camping and paddling, winter cross-country skiing and snowshoeing
attractions.tips: Arrive early on summer weekends as parking fills up quickly. All food must be stored in bear-resistant lockers at campsites. Propane/gas grills only — charcoal and wood fires are prohibited in day use areas. The park has no boat launch; use the nearby Truckee Donner Recreation & Parks District ramp for a fee. Dress in layers — summer days are warm but nights drop to the 40s, and winter is very cold. Free interpretive programs (paddling tours, snowshoe tours) are available through the Sierra State Parks Foundation.
attractions.nearby: Donner Lake, Truckee (historic downtown), Northstar California Resort, Sugar Bowl Ski Resort, Tahoe City, Emerald Bay State Park, Tahoe Rim Trail, Truckee River, Old Town Truckee

attractions.name: Donner Pass
attractions.description: Donner Pass is a 7,056-foot-high (2,151 m) mountain pass in the northern Sierra Nevada, located above Donner Lake and Donner Memorial State Park about 9 miles west of Truckee, California. It has been traversed by the California Trail, First Transcontinental Railroad, Lincoln Highway, US Route 40, and Interstate 80. The pass gets its name from the ill-fated Donner Party who overwintered there in 1846. Today, the area is home to a thriving recreational community with several alpine lakes and ski resorts including Donner Ski Ranch, Boreal, and Sugar Bowl.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 7,056-foot elevation mountain pass in the Sierra Nevada
Historic crossing point for the California Trail and First Transcontinental Railroad
Named after the ill-fated Donner Party of 1846
Home to multiple ski resorts: Boreal, Sugar Bowl, and Donner Ski Ranch
Access to alpine lakes and year-round recreational activities
Part of the Lincoln Highway and US Route 40 historic routes
attractions.tips: Donner Pass can experience heavy snowfall and hazardous driving conditions in winter months—carry tire chains and check road conditions before traveling. I-80 over the pass may close during severe storms. Visit in summer for hiking and alpine lake access, or in winter for skiing at nearby resorts. The Donner Memorial State Park visitor center nearby provides historical context about the Donner Party.
attractions.nearby: Donner Memorial State Park, Donner Ski Ranch, Boreal Mountain Resort, Sugar Bowl Resort, Donnor Lake, Soda Springs, Kingvale, Lake Tahoe

attractions.name: El Dorado Beach/Lakeview Commons
attractions.description: El Dorado Beach and Lakeview Commons is the largest beach area in South Lake Tahoe, located on Highway 50 at Lakeview Avenue. It features a boat ramp, swim area, picnic facilities, and a large turf area perfect for families. The beach also offers a kayak and water toy concession, bike trail access, and year-round accessibility depending on conditions.
attractions.address: 1004 Lakeview Avenue (Between Rufus Allen & Lakeview off Hwy. 50), South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
attractions.hours: Boat Ramp: Fridays–Wednesdays 9:00 AM–7:00 PM; Thursdays Closed. Last In & Out launch at 5:30 PM. Beach area open year-round depending on accessibility. Note: Boat ramp was closed for the 2025 season per the official website.
attractions.days_open: Fridays through Wednesdays (boat ramp); Beach area open year-round
attractions.admission: Free (parking is free; kayak/water toy concessions and food concessions available for purchase)
highlights: Largest beach area in South Lake Tahoe
Boat launch with floating dock and trailer parking
Roped and buoyed swim area
Large picnic area with tables and BBQ grills
Kayak and water toy concession
Bike and walking trail access
attractions.tips: The boat ramp is closed on Thursdays and the 4th of July. Last boat launch is at 5:30 PM. Motorized boats must have an intact wire inspection seal from TahoeBoatInspections.com — all boats must be Clean, Drained, and Dry. Maximum boat length is 23 feet and maximum height on trailer is 11 feet due to the bridge clearance. Boat trailer parking is limited to 38 spaces and overnight parking is prohibited.
attractions.nearby: Pioneer Trail, Campground by the Lake, Bijou Golf Course, Heavenly Mountain Resort, Tahoe Queen paddlewheel boat, South Lake Tahoe casinos, Tahoe Keys Boulevard shopping

attractions.name: Emerald Bay State Park
attractions.description: Located along Lake Tahoe's west shore, Emerald Bay State Park offers spectacular views of the blue-green water and features activities such as camping, hiking, boating, and swimming. The park was designated a National Natural Landmark for its brilliant panorama of mountain-building processes and glacier-carved granite. The natural beauty, geology, and history—including Vikingsholm Castle and Fannette Island—make it one of the highlights of the Lake Tahoe area. [California State Parks], Wikipedia
attractions.address: Highway 89, 12 miles north of South Lake Tahoe
attractions.hours: Sunrise to sunset daily (parking lot). Seasonal services and camping close in fall/winter (e.g., closed for 2025 season as of Sep 2025; check updates). [[CA State Parks](https://www.parks.ca.gov/?page_id=506)]
attractions.days_open: Open daily year-round, weather permitting (snow/avalanche closures possible in winter)
attractions.admission: $10 per vehicle day use ($3 for 1 hour). Self-pay (cash exact/credit). Annual passes accepted. [[CA State Parks](https://www.parks.ca.gov/?page_id=506)]
highlights: Spectacular views of Emerald Bay and Lake Tahoe
Vikingsholm Castle (Scandinavian architecture, summer tours)
Fannette Island (Lake Tahoe's only island)
Hiking trails including Rubicon Trail and Eagle Falls
National Natural Landmark and underwater park
Kayaking, boating, scuba diving, camping
attractions.tips: Arrive early (before 9 AM) for parking, especially weekends. No dogs on trails/beach. Vikingsholm trail is steep—assess fitness, bring water. Layer clothing; temps vary. Store food in bear lockers. Check Caltrans for road conditions in winter. [[CA State Parks](https://www.parks.ca.gov/?page_id=506)]
attractions.nearby: D.L. Bliss State Park (adjacent, north), Eagle Falls Trail (USFS), Sugar Pine Point State Park, South Lake Tahoe attractions

attractions.name: Explore Tahoe Visitor Center
attractions.description: The Explore Tahoe Visitor Center is the only interactive visitor center in the Lake Tahoe Basin, located in the Heavenly Village at the Nevada-California state line. Staffed with friendly, knowledgeable locals, it offers cultural history exhibits, interactive museum displays, free maps and brochures, interpretive programs, and tips for responsible recreation. It serves as the central hub for discovering all the best ways to enjoy the South Lake Tahoe region in every season.
attractions.address: 4114 Lake Tahoe Boulevard, South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
attractions.hours: Monday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Tuesday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Wednesday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Saturday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM, Sunday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM (Closed Christmas Day and Thanksgiving Day)
attractions.days_open: Daily (7 days a week), except Christmas Day and Thanksgiving Day
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Only interactive visitor center in the Lake Tahoe Basin
Located in the Heavenly Village at the Nevada-California state line
Interactive museum exhibits on Lake Tahoe's natural and cultural history
Free interpretive programs and responsible recreation tips
Access to public transit information around Lake Tahoe
Free maps, brochures, and visitor information specialists on staff
attractions.tips: Stop here first when arriving in South Lake Tahoe to get free maps, brochures, and local event information. The parking garage is located at 1 Bellamy Ct., South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150. The center is just steps from the Heavenly Village Gondola, making it easy to combine with other activities. Staff can provide recommendations for dining, lodging, hiking, biking, and seasonal activities.
attractions.nearby: Heavenly Village Shops & Restaurants, Heavenly Mountain Gondola, Lake Tahoe Beach (nearby), Tahoe Art League Art Center & Gallery, Lake Tahoe Historical Society Museum, El Dorado Beach, South Lake Tahoe Ice Arena, Stateline Nevada Casinos

attractions.name: Flume Trail
attractions.description: The Flume Trail is one of the most spectacular mountain biking and hiking trails in the world, carved into the steep granite cliffs of Lake Tahoe's east shore. The trail follows the historic path where wooden square-box flumes once delivered water to Virginia City during the Comstock Lode mining boom in the late 1800s. It features breathtaking views of Lake Tahoe from up to 1,600 feet above the shoreline, with the trail ranging between 7,000 and 8,157 feet in elevation.
attractions.hours: Open sunrise to sunset (trail is snow-free and accessible May to November, weather permitting)
attractions.days_open: Daily (May–November). Tahoe Rim Trail sections are open to mountain bikes only on even-numbered days of the week.
attractions.admission: $2 per person day-use entrance fee at Spooner Lake State Park. Shuttle service is $20 (includes $2 entrance fee) per person. Mountain bike rentals start at $55/day.
highlights: Spectacular singletrack carved into granite cliffs 1,600 feet above Lake Tahoe's east shore
Historic route following the original Comstock-era water flume to Virginia City
Stunning panoramic views of Lake Tahoe and surrounding Sierra Nevada mountains
14-mile point-to-point ride with 1,100 feet of climbing in the first 4 miles
Access to pristine Marlette Lake at 8,157 feet elevation
Commercial shuttle service available from Tunnel Creek Cafe in Incline Village
attractions.tips: Ride the trail from Spooner Lake to Incline Village (south to north) for a mostly downhill experience. Bring plenty of water, snacks, a repair kit, spare tube, bike pump, first aid, and sunblock. Wear a helmet at all times — there are sections with significant exposure and steep drop-offs. Consider using the Flume Trail Bikes shuttle service if you only have one car. The trail is best from May to November; check snow conditions before visiting. Arrive early as the trail can get crowded during peak season.
attractions.nearby: Spooner Lake State Park, Marlette Lake, Sand Harbor Beach, Tahoe Rim Trail, Chimney Beach, Cave Rock, Incline Village, Tunnel Creek Cafe, Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park, Mt. Rose Ski Tahoe

attractions.name: Heavenly Mountain Resort
attractions.description: Heavenly Mountain Resort is a premier ski and snowboard destination straddling the California-Nevada border in South Lake Tahoe. With 4,800 acres of terrain, 34 miles of groomed trails, and stunning views of Lake Tahoe, it is one of the largest and most diverse ski resorts in the Lake Tahoe area. Beyond winter sports, the resort offers summer activities including a scenic gondola ride, mountain coaster, zipline, and climbing wall.
attractions.hours: Skiing/Riding: 10am - 5pm (seasonal, currently open Sat 4/18 & Sun 4/19 as bonus weekend); Gondola Sightseeing: 9am - 4pm (seasonal); Mountain Coaster & Tubing: 10:30am - 4pm (seasonal); Gondola Ticket Office: 9:30am - 5pm. Hours vary by season—check website for current schedule.
attractions.days_open: Daily (seasonal operation; typically late November through mid-April for winter, May through mid-October for summer)
attractions.admission: Lift Tickets (day-of, subject to change): Adult (19-64) $186-$265, Child (5-12) $186, Junior (13-18) $239, Senior (65+) $239. Epic Day Pass from $96. Epic Full Season Pass from $1,089. Epic Local Pass $799. Summer Gondola Sightseeing: Adult $129, Child (5-12) $74, Teen/Senior $115.
highlights: 4,800 acres of skiable terrain across California and Nevada
34 miles of wide-open groomed cruisers with double-black-diamond canyons
Heavenly Gondola with scenic Lake Tahoe views
Mountain Coaster and Hot Shot Zipline for summer fun
Ski & Ride School offering lessons in ten languages
Epic Pass access to 37+ resorts across North America and Europe
attractions.tips: Buy lift tickets online in advance to save up to 50%. Parking reservations are required on weekends and holidays at the California Base Area. Arrive early to beat crowds—things get busy around 10-11am. Use the My Epic app for hands-free mobile lift tickets. Text 'UPDATES' to 530-302-2751 for real-time mountain conditions. Weekday visits are less crowded than weekends.
attractions.nearby: South Lake Tahoe casinos (Harveys, Harrah's), Emerald Bay State Park, Vikingsholm, Heavenly Village, Kirkwood Mountain Resort, Northstar California Resort, Lake Tahoe beaches, Tahoe Rim Trail, Stateline casinos on Nevada side

attractions.name: Heavenly Mountain Scenic Gondola
attractions.description: The Heavenly Mountain Scenic Gondola is a 2.4-mile scenic ride offering breathtaking panoramic views of Lake Tahoe from nearly 9,000 feet above sea level. Located at the Heavenly Mountain Resort in South Lake Tahoe, it operates year-round as a sightseeing attraction even when the slopes aren't open for skiing. The gondola travels between California and Nevada, providing access to the Tamarack Lodge at the mid-station.
attractions.hours: Saturday: 9 AM - 4 PM; Sunday: 9 AM - 4 PM; Monday: 9 AM - 4 PM; Tuesday: 9 AM - 4 PM; Wednesday: 9 AM - 4 PM; Thursday: 9 AM - 4 PM; Friday: 9 AM - 4 PM
attractions.admission: 1 Day Scenic Gondola (Sightseeing Only): Adult (Ages 19-64) $104, Teen (Ages 13-18) $92, Child (Ages 5-12) $71, Senior (Ages 65+) $92 — 1 Day Scenic Gondola + Lunch: Adult $130, Teen $118, Child $97, Senior $118 — 1 Day Scenic Gondola + 1 Activity: Adult $154, Teen $142, Child $106, Senior $142
highlights: 2.4-mile scenic gondola ride to 9,000 feet above sea level
Panoramic views of Lake Tahoe and surrounding mountains
Access to Tamarack Lodge at mid-station with dining and shopping
Year-round operation for sightseeing (summer and winter)
Crosses between California and Nevada states
Level walkway at mid-station with viewing platforms
attractions.tips: Purchase tickets online in advance for the best price and availability. Arrive early in the day for clearer views and shorter lines. From 3:30 PM the gondola only offers a ride-around without mid-station access. Dress in layers as it can be windy and cooler at higher elevations. Bring a camera for the stunning photo opportunities. Check weather conditions before visiting as fog can obscure views.
attractions.nearby: Heavenly Village (shopping, dining, entertainment), Lake Tahoe Beaches, Stateline Casinos (Harveys, Harrahs, MontBleu), Heavenly Mountain Resort ski slopes, Edgewood Tahoe Golf Course, Vikingsholm at Emerald Bay State Park, Taylor Creek Visitor Center

attractions.name: Historic Downtown Truckee
attractions.description: Historic Downtown Truckee is a charming Old West railroad town in the High Sierra, founded in 1863 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The Commercial Row – Brickelltown Historic District features late 19th-century brick architecture housing boutique shops, art galleries, restaurants, and museums. Originally a pivotal stop on the First Transcontinental Railroad and Gold Rush hub, today it serves as a gateway to Lake Tahoe and the Sierra Nevada mountains.
attractions.hours: Sunday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Monday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Tuesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Wednesday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Thursday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Friday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM, Saturday: 10:00 AM - 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free (parking fees may apply at certain attractions)
highlights: National Register of Historic Places-listed Commercial Row – Brickelltown Historic District
Historic Truckee Train Depot housing the Welcome Center and Museum of Truckee History
Old Truckee Jail Museum (built 1875) on Jibboom Street
Truckee Donner Railroad Society Caboose Museum
Self-guided historic walking tour of 9 geographic character areas
Charming boutique shops, art galleries, and restaurants in late 1800s brick buildings
attractions.tips: Parking can be limited during peak hours and weekends — plan accordingly or park in paid lots ($6/day). Visit during weekdays for a less crowded experience. Many shops may close early, so plan your visit for daytime. Don't miss Truckee Thursdays in summer when the main street is blocked off for vendors and a beer garden. Set aside 2-4 hours to fully explore the downtown area. The visitor center at the Train Depot provides walking tour maps and local recommendations.
attractions.nearby: Donner Memorial State Park, Truckee Railroad Museum, Old Truckee Jail Museum, Rocking Stone Tower, Truckee California Welcome Center, Donner Lake, Boreal Mountain Resort, Truckee River Legacy Trail

attractions.name: Incline Village and Crystal Bay
attractions.description: Incline Village and Crystal Bay are upscale communities on the north shore of Lake Tahoe in Washoe County, Nevada. Incline Village, established in 1882, is known for its luxury real estate, Diamond Peak Ski Resort, and pristine beaches along Lake Tahoe. Crystal Bay sits adjacent to the California state line and is famous for its casinos and proximity to Sand Harbor State Park. Together they form a premier year-round destination offering skiing, boating, hiking, golf, and world-class lake recreation at an elevation of 6,350 feet.
attractions.hours: Incline Village and Crystal Bay are open residential/resort communities accessible 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. The Incline Village Crystal Bay Visitors Bureau operates weekdays 8 a.m. to 5 p.m., weekends 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. (and some holidays).
attractions.admission: Free — no admission fee to visit the communities
highlights: Diamond Peak Ski Resort — community-owned ski area with lake views
9 private sandy beaches on Lake Tahoe with crystal-clear waters
Luxury real estate among the most expensive in the United States
Tahoe East Shore Trail — 3-mile paved trail to Sand Harbor State Park
Crystal Bay casinos and entertainment venues
20 community golf courses, recreation center, and events center
attractions.tips: Visit Sand Harbor State Park for the most beautiful beaches (parking fills early, use the TART shuttle). The Tahoe East Shore Trail offers stunning lake views and connects Incline Village to Sand Harbor. Diamond Peak Ski Resort is best visited in winter months (December-April). Summer is peak season — book lodging well in advance. The Incline Village Crystal Bay Visitors Bureau at 969 Tahoe Blvd provides maps, dining recommendations, and activity itineraries. Parking at beaches requires fees during summer months.
attractions.nearby: Sand Harbor State Park, Diamond Peak Ski Resort, Tahoe East Shore Trail, Flume Trail, University of Nevada Reno at Lake Tahoe, Crystal Bay Casino, Tahoe Rim Trail, Spooner Lake State Park, Mount Rose Ski Tahoe, Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival at Sand Harbor

attractions.name: Lake Tahoe Balloons
attractions.category: Hot Air Balloon Ride / Adventure Tour
attractions.description: Lake Tahoe Balloons offers the only hot air balloon rides over Lake Tahoe, launching and landing on the world's only U.S.C.G. certified balloon aircraft recovery vessel, the Tahoe Flyer. Operating from May through October, the experience includes a sunrise boat cruise, a 45-minute to 1-hour balloon flight over the lake and Sierra Nevada mountains, and a post-flight champagne toast. Guests enjoy panoramic views of Emerald Bay, Fallen Leaf Lake, Cascade Lake, the Desolation Wilderness, and on clear days, peaks in Yosemite over 150 miles away.
attractions.hours: Flights depart early morning shortly after sunrise, with meeting times varying by date from 5:30 AM to 7:00 AM. Office hours are 9:00 AM to 5:00 PM PST daily. The entire experience takes approximately 4-5 hours.
attractions.days_open: 7 days a week (May 10 – October 20, weather permitting)
attractions.admission: $399 per person (Classic Lake Tahoe Balloon Flight). Tahoe Locals Standby: $99 per person (South Lake Tahoe residents/employees, standby basis only). Group outings and VIP flights available at custom pricing.
highlights: Only hot air balloon operation in the world to inflate, launch, land, and deflate on a boat (the Tahoe Flyer)
45-minute to 1-hour flight reaching up to 10,000 feet elevation over Lake Tahoe
Panoramic views of Emerald Bay, Fallen Leaf Lake, Desolation Wilderness, and Yosemite peaks
Includes boat cruise, beverages, snacks, and traditional post-flight champagne toast
Impeccable safety record with experienced, professionally trained crew
Operates sunrise flights 7 days a week, weather permitting
attractions.tips: Dress in layers — early mornings can be in the 30s-40s°F even in summer. Wear comfortable walking shoes and a hat. Call the pre-flight weather check-in line no more than an hour before your scheduled meeting time to confirm your flight is proceeding. Bring a camera for breathtaking scenery, sunglasses, and sunscreen. Arrive on time — late arrivals are considered no-shows and charged in full. The entire experience takes 4-5 hours, so plan accordingly.
attractions.nearby: Emerald Bay State Park, Vikingsholm Castle, D.L. Bliss State Park, Heavenly Mountain Resort, Tahoe Keys Marina, Pope Beach, El Dorado Beach, Harveys Lake Tahoe, Harrah's Lake Tahoe

attractions.name: Lake Tahoe Historical Society Museum
attractions.description: The Lake Tahoe Historical Society Museum is a small history museum in South Lake Tahoe dedicated to preserving the region's heritage. Founded in 1968 by volunteers, it features exhibits on the Washoe Tribe, early farming and settlers, trains, boats, fashion, cameras, skiing, Pony Express, ranching, and Indian basketry. The museum grounds include the oldest standing commercial building in South Lake Tahoe — the Osgood Toll House — and a historic 1930s log cabin.
attractions.address: 3058 Lake Tahoe Blvd., South Lake Tahoe, CA 96150
attractions.hours: September to mid-December: Saturdays 11:00 AM – 3:00 PM (closed Saturday after Thanksgiving). January to May: Closed for winter. May: Saturdays 11:00 AM – 3:00 PM. June through August: Thursdays, Fridays, Saturdays 11:00 AM – 3:00 PM. The 1930s Log Cabin: Saturdays, June through August, 12:00 PM – 2:00 PM.
attractions.days_open: Thursdays, Fridays, Saturdays (seasonal — closed January through April and mid-December)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Displays on Washoe Tribe, early settlers, Pony Express, and ranching
Osgood Toll House — oldest standing commercial building in South Lake Tahoe
Historic 1930s Log Cabin with Christmas exhibits
Museum bookstore with books, DVDs, postcards, and gifts on Lake Tahoe history
Children's Programs and historic walking tours of Al Tahoe
Pioneer Cemetery and Tahoe Tree Ring Project
attractions.tips: Call ahead at (530) 541-5458 before visiting — the museum is volunteer-run and may open for private tours even during closed months. The museum is small, so plan 30-60 minutes for your visit. Visit during summer (June-August) for the most open days including the log cabin. Check their Facebook page for special events like the Festival of Winter Lights.
attractions.nearby: Heavenly Ski Resort, Lake Tahoe Beaches (Pope Beach, Baldwin Beach), Harveys Casino Resort, Edgewood Tahoe Resort, South Lake Tahoe Gondola, Taylor Creek Visitor Center

attractions.name: Mount Rose Ski Tahoe
attractions.description: Mount Rose Ski Tahoe (commonly known as Mount Rose) is a ski resort in Nevada situated in the Sierra Nevada mountains near Reno, Incline Village, and Lake Tahoe. Despite its name, the resort is actually on the slopes of Slide Mountain rather than Mount Rose. It is known for having the highest base elevation of any ski resort in the Lake Tahoe region at 8,260 feet (2,520 m), receiving an average of 350 inches of snowfall annually across 1,200 acres of terrain. The resort opened in 1953 as the "Reno Ski Bowl" and features over 70 trails with beginner, intermediate, advanced, and expert terrain.
attractions.hours: Winter Season: Full-Day lifts 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM (Fridays & Saturdays open until 6:00 PM); Morning Half-Day 9:00 AM – 12:30 PM; Afternoon Half-Day 12:30 PM – 4:00 PM. Zephyr Express opens at 8:30 AM for season pass holders. Summer: Main Lodge Monday–Thursday 8:00 AM – 4:30 PM (by appointment, call ahead). Winters Creek Lodge open for events only.
attractions.days_open: Daily during winter season (conditions permitting); Summer operations limited to select days by appointment
attractions.admission: Full-Day Adult (16+): $189 full price / discounted online when purchased by midnight the day before. Full-Day Child (6–15): $95 full price / discounted online. Tyke (5 & under): $20 at resort only. Morning or Afternoon Half-Day tickets available at reduced rates. Friday Afternoon (after 12 PM): $79 (G.I.F. ticket). Season passes also available.
highlights: Highest base elevation of any Lake Tahoe ski resort at 8,260 feet
Over 1,200 acres of skiable terrain with 70+ trails
The Chutes – some of the steepest and most consistent expert terrain in Tahoe
Two distinct mountain areas: Slide side (advanced) and Rose side (beginner-friendly)
Average annual snowfall of 350 inches
Friday and Saturday late-night skiing until 6 PM with music, food, and drinks at the view deck
attractions.tips: Purchase lift tickets online by midnight the day before for significant discounts versus walk-up window prices. Check weather conditions before visiting as the high elevation can result in wind holds and harsh conditions. Fridays and Saturdays offer extended hours until 6 PM with a festive atmosphere. The resort is only about 25 miles from downtown Reno, making it the closest ski area to the city. Arrive early on powder days as parking can fill quickly.
attractions.nearby: Lake Tahoe (10 miles), Incline Village, Diamond Peak Ski Resort, Palisades Tahoe (formerly Squaw Valley/Alpine Meadows), Sand Harbor State Park, Nevada State Route 431 (Mount Rose Scenic Byway), Tahoe Rim Trail, Reno (25 miles)

attractions.name: Nevada Beach Campground
attractions.description: Nevada Beach Campground sits on the southeast shore of Lake Tahoe in the Lake Tahoe Basin of Nevada, nestled among native pine trees near the sandy beach. It offers dozens of tent and RV campsites with lake views, picnic tables, campfire rings, and grills. The campground is managed by the Lake Tahoe Basin Management Unit (U.S. Forest Service) and operated by a private concessionaire, Tahoe Recreation. The nearby Nevada Beach is one of the widest and most scenic beaches on the lake, popular for swimming, kayaking, and water sports.
attractions.hours: Open seasonally May 22 - October 12, 2026. Check-in: 2:00 p.m., Check-out: 12:00 p.m. (noon). Gated access.
attractions.admission: Campsite (Standard Nonelectric): $47.00 per night (Peak Season). $2.00 additional holiday fee per night. Extra vehicle fee applies ($7 per night for additional vehicles). Day use entry fee required at the beach.
highlights: 54 campsites for tents and RVs (up to 60 feet)
Half-mile of beautiful sandy beach along Lake Tahoe
Lake views from many campsites
Group pavilion available for rent (up to 150 people)
Kayak and paddleboard rentals on-site
Food concession stand during summer months
attractions.tips: Reservations are highly recommended and should be made through Recreation.gov up to 6 months in advance. Bears frequent the area—use food storage lockers provided at each site. Pets are allowed on leash in the campground but not on the beach or in picnic areas. Fire restrictions may be imposed during hot, dry conditions. The beach is windy, which helps with bugs but can be strong for kiteboarding. South end of the beach is dog-friendly. Extra vehicles will be charged a fee at the campground.
attractions.nearby: Stateline casinos and restaurants, Kiva Beach, Lam Wa Tah Nature Trail, Round Hill Pines Beach, Zephyr Cove Marina, Spooner Lake State Park, Emerald Bay State Park, Heavenly Ski Resort

attractions.name: Northstar California Resort
attractions.description: Northstar California (formerly Northstar-at-Tahoe) is a premier mountain resort located near the northwest shore of Lake Tahoe in Placer County, California. Part of the Vail Resorts Epic Pass family, it offers world-class skiing and snowboarding during winter, plus mountain biking, golf, and other outdoor activities in summer. The resort features a charming European-style village with dining, shopping, ice skating, and family-friendly amenities.
attractions.hours: Winter Season: Closed for 2025-2026. Summer operations TBD. Season Pass & Ticket Office (April 2026): Friday-Sunday 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM
attractions.days_open: Winter: Daily (seasonal). Summer: Daily (seasonal). Currently closed for winter 2025-2026.
attractions.admission: Lift tickets vary by day and demand, ranging from approximately $127-$199 for a 1-day adult ticket. Season passes (Epic Pass) available starting at $555 for 2026-2027. Prices subject to change; purchase online for best rates.
highlights: Over 3,170 acres of skiable terrain with 100+ trails across 6 mountain peaks
Gondola One - a 4-minute gondola ride from the Village to the mountain summit
Terrain parks for all skill levels including one of the largest in the Sierra Nevada
European-style Village at Northstar with ice skating rink, shops, restaurants, and bars
Family-friendly activities including tubing, snow play, and kids' ski school programs
Summer operations featuring mountain biking, golf, hiking, and zip-lining
attractions.tips: Book lift tickets and lodging in advance for the best rates, especially on weekends and holidays. Check the snow report and weather before visiting. The resort is currently closed for the 2025-2026 winter season but will reopen for summer operations. Parking can be expensive; consider using the free shuttle from designated parking areas. Arrive early on powder days to avoid long lift lines.
attractions.nearby: Palisades Tahoe (formerly Squaw Valley), Sugar Bowl Ski Resort, Downtown Truckee, Donner Lake, Lake Tahoe, Northstar Village

attractions.name: Palisades Tahoe (formerly Squaw Valley Alpine Meadows)
attractions.description: Palisades Tahoe is a premier ski resort located in Olympic Valley on the North Shore of Lake Tahoe. Home to the 1960 Winter Olympics, it features 3,600 skiable acres across two mountains with an average annual snowfall of 400 inches. The resort includes 187 trails, 26 lifts, and a peak elevation of 9,050 feet, making it one of the largest ski resorts in North America.
attractions.hours: Winter: 9am-4pm (November – May, subject to change); Summer: June – Mid-September (hours vary)
attractions.admission: Lift tickets from $79/day for passholders; full-day lift tickets vary by date and advance purchase. Ikon Pass accepted.
highlights: Host of the 1960 Winter Olympics with Olympic heritage
3,600 skiable acres across two mountains (Palisades and Alpine)
Average annual snowfall of 400 inches with over 300 sunny days per year
26 lifts including a 110-person aerial tram
187 trails ranging from beginner to expert, with 16 bowls
Peak elevation of 9,050 feet with vertical rise of 2,850 feet
attractions.tips: Check the Mountain Report before visiting as hours and lift operations are weather-dependent. Purchase lift tickets in advance for better rates (starting at $79/day). The resort operates November through May for winter and June through mid-September for summer. Parking can fill up on weekends—arrive early. The 110-person aerial tram offers stunning views even for non-skiers. Ikon Pass holders get access and discounts.
attractions.nearby: Northstar California Resort, Lake Tahoe State Park, Tahoe City, Truckee, Emerald Bay State Park, Scotty's Castle area, Nevada Beach, Sand Harbor

attractions.name: Pope Beach
attractions.description: Pope Beach is a popular sandy beach located on National Forest land along the southwestern shore of Lake Tahoe, approximately 3 miles west of South Lake Tahoe. The beach stretches approximately three-quarters of a mile with a sandy shoreline, offering excellent swimming, picnicking, and paddleboarding opportunities. Nestled among native pine trees, it features a paved trail through the forest leading to the beach and is known for its crystal-clear water and family-friendly atmosphere.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to sunset (open daily during season)
attractions.admission: $10 per vehicle per day (parking fee); season passes available for purchase at the kiosk
highlights: 3/4 mile of sandy beach with crystal-clear Lake Tahoe water
Excellent swimming and picnicking with picnic tables and shade from pine trees
Day use only with $10 vehicle parking fee
Located on National Forest land with paved forest trail access
Paddleboarding and kayaking opportunities
Food concessions available during summer season
attractions.tips: Arrive by 9 AM on weekends and before 11 AM on weekdays to secure parking. No dogs or charcoal grills allowed (propane grills are permitted). The water is very cold even in summer. Parking can fill up quickly during peak season. Walking or biking in can help avoid parking fees.
attractions.nearby: Tallac Historic Site (0.9 mi), Taylor Creek Visitor Center (1.2 mi), Camp Richardson (0.5 mi), Valhalla at Lake Tahoe (0.6 mi), Emerald Bay State Park, Mt. Tallac Trail (1.8 mi), Inspiration Point Vista, Fallen Leaf Lake

attractions.name: Round Hill Pines Beach Resort
attractions.description: Round Hill Pines Beach Resort is a half-mile long sandy beach located on National Forest land on the East Shore of Lake Tahoe near Zephyr Cove, Nevada. The resort offers a secluded beach with panoramic views of Mt. Tallac, Emerald Bay, and the West Shore, plus a lakeview restaurant, beach bar, marina with watercraft rentals, and wedding and group event facilities. Open seasonally from May through October, it is a popular family-friendly destination for swimming, sunbathing, and water sports.
attractions.hours: Open May through October, 8:00 AM until sunset (seasonal). Closed for the season from mid-October through April.
attractions.admission: Day use parking fee: $12 per vehicle per day. FREE parking after 5:00 PM. Beach access included with parking fee.
highlights: Half-mile sandy beach with panoramic views of Mt. Tallac and Emerald Bay
Lakeview restaurant (Beach Bums Bar & Grill) and beachside bar (The Rum Bar)
Marina offering boat, jet ski, kayak, SUP, and pedal boat rentals
Beach volleyball courts and wedding/event venue with panoramic lake views
Tahoe Serenity yacht cruises departing from the marina
Voted 2024 Tahoe's Best Place to Watch the Sunset
attractions.tips: Arrive early, especially on weekends, as the parking lot fills up quickly. Parking is free after 5:00 PM. Bring cash for the $12 parking fee. No pets allowed anywhere on the property. No glass containers or charcoal/wood fires permitted on the beach. There is no lifeguard on duty — lake water can be very cold. Reserve watercraft rentals online in advance. The wind picks up in the afternoon, making kayaking more challenging after 1:30 PM.
attractions.nearby: Nevada Beach, Van Sickle Bi-State Park, The Gondola at Heavenly, Sand Harbor Beach, Lakeside Beach, Pope Beach, Harveys Lake Tahoe Casino, Brooks' Bar & Deck, Edgewood Restaurant, Rabe Meadows

attractions.name: Sand Harbor State Park (Lake Tahoe–Nevada State Park)
attractions.description: Sand Harbor is a stunning state park on the east shore of Lake Tahoe, featuring a large sandy beach, crystal-clear waters, and dramatic rock formations. It is home to the popular Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival and offers hiking, swimming, kayaking, and picnicking. The park covers 55 acres and is part of the larger Lake Tahoe–Nevada State Park system, which spans approximately 14,301 acres across multiple management units.
attractions.hours: 8:00 a.m. to 1 hour after sunset. Boat Ramp: Closed for the season.
attractions.admission: Day use: $10.00 per vehicle (Nevada vehicles), $15.00 per vehicle (Non-NV vehicles). Boat launch: $20.00 (NV), $25.00 (Non-NV), currently closed for season. Bike in: $2.00 per bike.
highlights: Large sandy beach with crystal-clear Lake Tahoe water and iconic rock formations
Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival every summer with performances on an outdoor stage
Tahoe East Shore Trail — dubbed 'America's Most Beautiful Bikeway'
Sand Point Nature Trail — handicap-accessible interpretive trail
Boat launch with two wave-protected double ramps and docks
Visitor Center with gift shop and Kokanee Bar and Grill concession
attractions.tips: Day-use reservations are required for vehicle entry May 1–September 30 (8:00am–10:30am, seven days a week May 15–September 30). Arrive early as the park fills up quickly during peak season — consider using the Tahoe Transportation District's East Shore Express shuttle. No glass bottles allowed in waterfront areas. Pets are only allowed off-season (October 15–April 15) and must be leashed. Practice Leave No Trace principles. No camping is permitted. Parking can be extremely limited during summer months.
attractions.nearby: Cave Rock State Park, Spooner Lake & Backcountry, Van Sickle Bi-State Park, Tahoe East Shore Trail, Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival grounds, Incline Village beaches

attractions.name: Sand Harbor State Park (formerly Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park)
attractions.description: Sand Harbor State Park, formerly known as Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park, is located on the northeastern shore of Lake Tahoe on the Nevada side. It features pristine beaches with crystal-clear water, dramatic granite boulders, and is one of the most popular day-use parks in the Nevada State Parks system. The park offers swimming, boating, hiking, kayaking, and hosts the annual Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival. Established in 1958, it is the largest alpine park in Nevada.
attractions.hours: 8:00 a.m. to 1 hour after sunset
attractions.admission: Day use: $10.00 per vehicle ($15.00 for Non-NV vehicles). Boat launch: $20.00 ($25.00 Non-NV). Bike in: $2.00 per bike. Day-use reservations required May 1–September 30, 8:00 a.m.–10:30 a.m.
highlights: Pristine beaches with crystal-clear Lake Tahoe water
Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival held every summer (July–August)
Tahoe East Shore Trail — dubbed 'America's Most Beautiful Bikeway'
Handicap-accessible Sand Point Nature Trail with interpretive signs
Group Area/Ramada accommodating up to 120 people for events
Kayaking, stand-up paddleboarding, and scuba diving opportunities
attractions.tips: Day-use reservations are required for vehicle entry from 8:00 a.m. to 10:30 a.m. daily, May 1 through September 30 — book in advance at ReserveNevada.com. From Memorial Day to Labor Day, the park experiences very high visitation; consider using the Tahoe Transportation District East Shore Express shuttle. No glass bottles are allowed in waterfront areas. No camping is permitted. Pets are only allowed off-season (October 15 to April 15) and must be leashed. The boat ramp is closed for the season (typically fall/winter). Arrive early to secure parking and beach space.
attractions.nearby: Cave Rock State Park, Spooner Lake & Backcountry, Van Sickle Bi-State Park, Diamond Peak Ski Resort, Lake Tahoe Shakespeare Festival venue, Incline Village casinos

attractions.name: Spooner Lake & Backcountry State Park
attractions.description: Spooner Lake & Backcountry State Park is nestled in the Lake Tahoe Basin, offering a widespread recreational and natural oasis with a lake surrounded by aspens and more than 12,000 acres of forested open space. The park features 50 miles of hiking, equestrian, and mountain biking trails, including the famous Marlette Flume Trail and Tahoe Rim Trail. Visitors can enjoy primitive camping, backcountry cabin rentals, fishing, cross-country skiing, and snowshoeing in a non-motorized wilderness area established in 1969.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to Sunset, 365 days a year
attractions.admission: $10.00 per vehicle (Nevada vehicles); $15.00 per vehicle (Non-Nevada vehicles); $2.00 per bike (bike-in); $2.00 per additional bike for vehicles with more than 4 bikes; Cabin rental fees available upon request from the park
highlights: 50 miles of hiking, equestrian, and mountain biking trails including the Tahoe Rim Trail and Marlette Flume Trail
Two backcountry cabins available for rent: Spooner Lake Cabin (sleeps 4) and Wildcat Cabin (sleeps 2)
Three primitive walk-in campgrounds: Marlette Peak, Hobart, and North Canyon
Fishing at Spooner Lake (limit 5, bait allowed) and seasonal catch-and-release at Marlette Lake
Cross-country skiing and snowshoeing with Nevada Nordic groomed trails in winter
Spooner Lake Visitor Center and Amphitheater serving as a hub for educational programs and ranger-led hikes
attractions.tips: The backcountry is non-motorized; only official agency vehicles or permitted State Parks vehicles are allowed. Pack out all trash as none is collected in the backcountry. Pets must be on a leash no longer than 6 feet. Drones are not allowed. Keep Tahoe bears wild by properly storing food. A Nevada fishing license is required. Check road conditions at nvroads.com before visiting, especially in winter. SCUP applications are only accepted October 1 – April 15.
attractions.nearby: Sand Harbor State Park, Cave Rock State Park, Marlette Lake, Hobart Reservoir, Tahoe Rim Trail, Van Sickle Bi-State Park, Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park

attractions.name: Sugar Bowl Ski Resort
attractions.description: Sugar Bowl is a historic ski and snowboard resort located on Donner Summit near Norden, California, in the Lake Tahoe region. Founded in 1939 by Austrian ski champion Hannes Schroll, it was California's first chairlift-equipped ski area and the first on the west coast to install a gondola lift. The resort features 1,650 acres of terrain across four peaks (Mt. Judah, Mt. Lincoln, Mt. Disney, and Crows Nest) with an average of 500 inches of annual snowfall.
attractions.hours: Seasonal: Opens approximately early December, closes early April (2025-26 season: 12/6/25 - 4/5/26). Typical operating hours: Main Ticket Windows 7:00am-12:00pm, Guest Services/Snowsports School 8:00am-4:30pm, Rentals 8:00am-5:00pm, Main Lodge 8:00am-5:00pm, Village Lodge 7:30am-9:00pm. Food & beverage venues vary (e.g., Judah Café 8:00am-4:00pm, Belt Room 10:00am-9:00pm). Hours subject to change based on conditions.
attractions.days_open: Daily during ski season (approximately mid-December through early April), subject to weather and snow conditions
attractions.admission: Lift tickets are date-specific and sold online or at the resort. Online advance purchase pricing (Sun-Fri starting rates and Sat/Holiday starting rates vary by date; up to 50% off when booked early vs. window rates). Window rates are higher. Reserved parking at Main Lodge Upper Lot: $35 on weekends and holidays. Season passes also available. First Time Ski & Ride Pack: $99 (beginner lesson included).
highlights: 1,650 acres of skiable terrain across four peaks with 105 marked trails
California's first chairlift (installed 1939) and west coast's first gondola
Average 500 inches of annual snowfall
Historic Tyrolean-style village with slopeside lodging
17% Beginner, 45% Intermediate, 38% Advanced terrain mix
Home to the annual Silver Bowl Race since 1940
attractions.tips: Book lift tickets online in advance to save up to 50% compared to window rates. The resort sits at high elevation on Donner Summit, so check road conditions on I-80 before visiting — chains may be required. Parking in the Upper Lot near Main Lodge requires a $35 reservation on weekends and holidays. The resort is closed during summer; planning a winter visit between mid-December and early April is recommended. Rent equipment slopeside for convenience.
attractions.nearby: Donner Lake, Donner Ski Ranch, Royal Gorge Cross Country Ski Resort, Sugar Rush Tubing, Truckee (nearby town with dining and lodging), Lake Tahoe North Shore (approx. 30 minutes south)

attractions.name: Tahoe Adventure Company
attractions.description: Tahoe Adventure Company is a premier outfitter and guided adventure company serving the North Lake Tahoe and Truckee areas for over 20 years. They offer a wide range of outdoor adventures including kayaking, stand-up paddleboarding, mountain biking, hiking, snowshoeing, and rock climbing. The company also provides kayak and SUP rentals, team building programs, corporate group events, and outdoor education programs.
attractions.hours: May - October: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM, 7 days/week; October - April: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM, 5 days/week
attractions.days_open: Year-round (7 days/week in summer, 5 days/week in winter)
attractions.admission: Varies by activity. Guided adventure tours typically range from $75-$150+ per person. Kayak and SUP rentals available at daily rates. Contact (530) 913-9212 or visit website for current pricing on specific trips.
highlights: Guided kayak, SUP, and clear kayak tours on Lake Tahoe
Mountain biking adventures on iconic trails like the Flume Trail
Year-round snowshoe tours and winter adventures
Stand-up paddleboard and kayak rentals with delivery service
Corporate team building and custom group outings
Outdoor education programs for schools
attractions.tips: Book tours in advance, especially during peak summer season. Check your confirmation email for specific meeting locations, as tours depart from various spots around North Lake Tahoe. The Tahoe Vista Recreation Area concession is for rentals only, not all tour meeting points. Bring sunscreen, water, and appropriate clothing for the season.
attractions.nearby: Crystal Bay Casino, Tahoe Vista State Recreation Area, Sand Harbor State Park, Mount Rose Ski Tahoe, Northstar California Resort, Flume Trail, Kings Beach

attractions.name: Tahoe East Shore Trail
attractions.description: Dubbed "America's Most Beautiful Bikeway," the Tahoe East Shore Trail is a spectacular 3-mile paved path connecting Incline Village to Sand Harbor State Park along Lake Tahoe's eastern Nevada shoreline. Open since June 2019, the 10-foot wide trail accommodates hikers, runners, and bicyclists with 11 beach access points and 25 interpretive panels highlighting the area's history, geology, flora, and fauna.
attractions.hours: Sunrise to Sunset
attractions.days_open: Open year-round (best experienced April through November)
attractions.admission: Free (trail access). Paid parking required at three parking lots along State Route 28 near Ponderosa Ranch Road; proceeds go toward trail maintenance. Sand Harbor State Park entry requires separate park admission fee.
highlights: Dubbed 'America's Most Beautiful Bikeway' – 3 miles of paved lakeside path
11 public beach access points along the scenic eastern shoreline
25 interpretive panels showcasing Lake Tahoe's history, geology, flora, and fauna
ADA-compliant 10-foot wide path accessible to all visitors
Connects Incline Village to Sand Harbor State Park with stunning alpine lake views
Year-round access with 30 bike racks and rest stops along the trail
attractions.tips: Start early in the morning (before 9 AM) during summer to avoid crowds and heat, as the trail has minimal shade. Bring plenty of water and apply sunscreen. Cyclists must yield to pedestrians; walk on the right and pass on the right. Dogs are welcome but must be leashed; be mindful that bridges can get very hot on warm days (wagons are available at each end for dog transport). Consider parking at Sand Harbor and walking north to avoid the steeper climb on the way back. The Tunnel Creek Cafe at the northern trailhead offers food and is a starting point for the Monkey Rock hike.
attractions.nearby: Sand Harbor State Park, Secret Cove Beach, Burnt Cedar Beach, Diamond Peak Ski Resort, Flume Trail, Monkey Rock Trailhead, Thunderbird Lake Tahoe, Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park, UC Davis Tahoe Environmental Research Center, Incline Beach

attractions.name: Tahoe Rim Trail
attractions.description: The Tahoe Rim Trail is a 165-mile scenic hiking and mountain biking loop that circumnavigates Lake Tahoe, passing through both California and Nevada. Established in 1981, it offers diverse terrain including alpine meadows, lake views, and forested ridgelines. The trail is maintained by the nonprofit Tahoe Rim Trail Association and is open for hiking, biking, and horseback riding seasonally.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily (seasonal access — typically May through October for full trail access; winter conditions may limit access)
attractions.days_open: Open year-round with seasonal access restrictions
attractions.admission: Free to access; some trailhead parking may require a fee. Backcountry camping permits required for overnight stays.
highlights: 165-mile loop trail encircling Lake Tahoe
Suitable for hiking, mountain biking, and horseback riding
Spectacular alpine and lake views throughout
Guided hikes and youth backcountry camps offered
Volunteer trail-building and maintenance programs
Multiple access points and trailheads around the lake
attractions.tips: Check current trail conditions for snow coverage and closures before visiting. Bring plenty of water — water sources are limited in sections. Arrive early on weekends as popular trailheads fill up. A backcountry permit is required for overnight camping. The full loop typically takes 10-14 days to complete; plan shuttle logistics if thru-hiking.
attractions.nearby: Lake Tahoe, Desolation Wilderness, Tahoe City, South Lake Tahoe, Spooner Lake State Park, Echo Lakes

attractions.name: Tahoe Science Center
attractions.description: The Tahoe Science Center is Lake Tahoe's only science center, operated by UC Davis Tahoe Environmental Research Center. Located in Incline Village, it features interactive exhibits on the lake ecosystem, geology, ecology, and environmental sustainability. Visitors can explore aquariums with native and invasive species, a 3-D theater, an interactive sandbox watershed model, and a virtual research boat.
attractions.hours: Labor Day through Memorial Day: Tuesdays-Fridays 11am and 12:30pm (guided tours), Self-guided 1:30pm-4:30pm. Memorial Day through Labor Day: Tuesdays-Fridays and Saturdays by request 11am and 12:30pm (guided tours). Closed Mondays and Sundays.
attractions.days_open: Tuesday through Friday (Saturdays by request during summer season)
attractions.admission: $5 - $10 per person
highlights: Interactive touchscreen exhibits on Lake Tahoe's ecosystem and environmental changes
Virtual research boat experience and virtual ecology lab
Aquariums featuring native and invasive species
Interactive sandbox watershed model where visitors create mountains and valleys
3-D theater showing the formation of Lake Tahoe
Green building tour showcasing sustainable design features
attractions.tips: Reserve timed tours in advance as they are required for guided visits. Arrive 10 minutes early to park in designated visitor spots. Free parking is available with a Temporary Visitor Parking Permit. Check the website for holiday closures including Winter Break, MLK Day, Presidents' Day, and other holidays. Virtual tours are available online for those who cannot visit in person.
attractions.nearby: Diamond Peak Ski Resort, Incline Village Beach, Sand Harbor State Park, Crystal Bay Casino, UC Davis Tahoe Environmental Research Center Tahoe City Field Station

attractions.name: Tallac Historic Site
attractions.description: The Tallac Historic Site consists of three historic estates built between 1894 and 1923 along the shores of Lake Tahoe. Once private summer retreats for affluent San Francisco families, including the Pope, Baldwin, and Heller (Valhalla) estates, the site was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1987. Today it serves as a free-to-visit museum and interpretive center managed by the Great Basin Institute in partnership with the Lake Tahoe Basin Management Unit.
attractions.hours: Museum, cabin exhibits, and guided tours are closed for the 2025 season. 2026 season opens Saturday, May 23, 2026. Grounds and trails remain accessible year-round during daylight hours. Specific museum hours for 2026 will be announced closer to opening.
attractions.days_open: Grounds open year-round; Museum and guided tours operate seasonally (approximately late May through early fall)
attractions.admission: Free. The Taylor Creek Visitor Center and Tallac Historic Site grounds are completely free to visit. Guided tours (Pope House Tour, Grand Estates Tour) are available for a fee via SimpleTix ticketing.
highlights: Three historic estates: Pope House, Baldwin Museum, and Valhalla (Heller Estate)
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1987
Guided tours including Pope House Tour and Grand Estates Tour
Scenic lakeside trails and grounds open year-round
Tallac Museum with maps, information, and program schedules
Beautiful historic architecture from the late 1800s and early 1900s
attractions.tips: The grounds and trails are free and open year-round, but museum exhibits and guided tours are only available during the summer season (typically late May through early fall). Check the website before visiting for current season dates. Bring water and wear comfortable shoes for walking the estate grounds. The site is about 3 miles northwest of South Lake Tahoe. Parking is available on-site.
attractions.nearby: Taylor Creek Visitor Center (adjacent), Pope Beach, Fallen Leaf Lake, Emerald Bay State Park, Vikingsholm Castle, Heavenly Mountain Resort, South Lake Tahoe casinos

attractions.name: Vikingsholm
attractions.description: Vikingsholm is a 38-room mansion on the shore of Emerald Bay at Lake Tahoe, built in 1929 by Lora Josephine Knight as a summer home. It is one of the finest examples of Scandinavian architecture in North America and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. The castle is part of Emerald Bay State Park and offers guided tours during the summer months.
attractions.hours: Tours: 10:30 AM – 4:00 PM (half-hourly); Visitor Center: 10:00 AM – 4:30 PM. Parking lot open sunrise to sunset.
attractions.admission: Tour tickets: $18 Adults; $15 Students (7-17, college ID), Active Duty Military, Seniors; Free for children 6 and under. Parking: $10 per vehicle per day. Tickets purchased at the Visitor Center.
highlights: 38-room Scandinavian-style mansion built in 1929
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places
Stunning location on the shores of Emerald Bay at Lake Tahoe
Guided 30-minute tours by knowledgeable Sierra State Parks Foundation guides
One-mile steep hiking trail through beautiful forest to reach the castle
Part of Emerald Bay State Park, a National Natural Landmark
attractions.tips: Arrive early (before 9 AM) to secure parking as the 60-vehicle lot fills quickly, especially on weekends. The hike down is steep but manageable; the return climb is 400 ft elevation gain over one mile. Wear comfortable shoes and bring water. Tours run only Memorial Day through September 30. Overnight parking is not allowed. Dogs are not allowed on the trail or in the castle. Alternative parking is available at the Eagle Falls trailhead (free).
attractions.nearby: Fannette Island, Emerald Bay Overlook, Eagle Falls, D.L. Bliss State Park, Rubicon Trail, Emerald Bay Visitor Center

attractions.name: Zephyr Cove Resort
attractions.description: Zephyr Cove Resort is a full-service lakeside resort located on National Forest land on Lake Tahoe's southeast shore. The resort features 28 individual cabins, a campground, marina with lake cruises, beach bar and grill, golf course, and a private beach. It offers a range of recreational activities including jet ski rentals, paddleboarding, beach volleyball, fishing, and mini golf, making it ideal for families and outdoor enthusiasts.
attractions.hours: Beach: Sunrise to Sunset (year-round). Front desk: Daily 11:00 AM - 8:00 PM. Check-in: 3:00 PM - 8:00 PM. Check-out: Before 11:00 AM.
attractions.admission: Beach entry fee required (varies by season; parking $12/day for non-guests in summer, free for resort guests; no parking fees in winter). Cabin rentals: $175-$600 per night.
highlights: 28 individual cabins and campground accommodations
Private beach on Lake Tahoe with sunrise-to-sunset access
Full-service marina with lake cruises and watercraft rentals
On-site golf course and mini golf
Beach bar and grill restaurant
Beach volleyball, jet ski rentals, and paddleboarding
attractions.tips: Arrive early in summer as beach parking fills quickly; resort guests get free parking. Beach is open year-round but facilities are seasonal. Book cabins well in advance for summer and holiday weekends. No campfires permitted on the beach. Contactless check-in/check-out available.
attractions.nearby: Edgewood Tahoe Golf Course, Nevada Beach, Lake Tahoe Nevada State Park, Heavenly Ski Resort, Stateline casinos (Golden Nugget, Harrah's, Hard Rock)

attractions.name: Anaho Island National Wildlife Refuge
attractions.description: Anaho Island National Wildlife Refuge is located within Pyramid Lake in Nevada and provides safe nesting habitat for one of the largest colonies of American white pelicans in the western United States. Established in 1913 by President Woodrow Wilson, the refuge protects this important bird colony and surrounding desert ecosystem. The refuge is closed to public access, but visitors can view the island from Pyramid Lake with proper tribal permits.
attractions.address: 3175 Freeman Lane, c/o Stillwater NWRC, Fallon, NV 89406
attractions.hours: The refuge is closed to all public access. Boats are prohibited within one thousand feet of the island.
attractions.admission: Not applicable — refuge is closed to public access. Tribal permits required for Pyramid Lake visitation, available online at plpt.nagfa.net or at the gas station in Nixon, NV.
highlights: One of the largest colonies of American white pelicans in the western United States
Established in 1913 by President Woodrow Wilson
Located within the Pyramid Lake Paiute Reservation
Part of the Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge Complex
Important desert ecosystem habitat
Home to diverse migratory bird species
attractions.tips: The refuge is closed to public access with boats prohibited within 1,000 feet of the island. You can view Anaho Island from Pyramid Lake if you obtain a tribal permit from the Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe (available online or at the gas station in Nixon, NV). The best viewing is from the north shore of Pyramid Lake. Bring binoculars or a telephoto lens for wildlife viewing. Contact the refuge at stillwater@fws.gov or (775) 423-5128 for more information.
attractions.nearby: Pyramid Lake State Recreation Area, Sutcliffe Visitor Center, Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe Museum, Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge, Fallon National Wildlife Refuge

attractions.name: Pyramid Lake
attractions.description: Pyramid Lake is the largest natural lake in Nevada, covering 125,000 acres as the biggest remnant of ancient Lake Lahontan. Located entirely within the Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe Reservation, 35 miles northeast of Reno, it is fed by the Truckee River and known for its striking blue-gray waters, unique tufa rock formations like the Pyramid and Stone Mother, and as a sacred site for the Paiute people.
