World-renowned for fishing Lahontan cutthroat trout and endangered Cui-ui fish, it also hosts a major colony of American white pelicans.Pyramid Lake official site, Wikipedia)
attractions.hours: Open daily from sunrise to sunset for day use (with permit); Ranger Station Mon-Fri 8am-4pm. Museum: Oct-Apr Mon-Fri 10am-4:30pm; May-Sep Mon-Sat 10am-4:30pm.[Pyramid Lake official site](https://pyramidlake.us/museum), [Permits](https://pyramidlake.us/permits)
attractions.days_open: Daily access with permit; some facilities weekdays only
attractions.admission: Day Use permit required: $25/vehicle daily (sunrise-sunset), $65/3-day; Camping $35/night per vehicle; Boating $30 daily; Fishing $27 daily (Oct-Jun). Buy online or at Ranger Station.[Pyramid Lake permits](https://pyramidlake.us/permits)
highlights: Largest natural lake in Nevada (125,000 acres)
Home to endangered Cui-ui fish and record Lahontan cutthroat trout
Sacred to Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe with unique tufa formations (Pyramid, Stone Mother)
Major breeding site for American white pelicans
Stunning desert scenery with color-changing waters
World-class fishing destination
attractions.tips: Purchase permits online in advance; respect closed areas (Needles, Anaho Island, Pyramid/Stone Mother); pack out all trash (no collection services); secure loads to prevent debris; be courteous to tribal members and observe speed limits; check for updates on regulations and golden mussel closures.[Pyramid Lake official site](https://pyramidlake.us)
attractions.nearby: Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe Museum & Visitors Center in Nixon (cultural exhibits, permits), Pyramid Lake Lodge (cabins, camping), Reno (40 miles south, casinos, nightlife), Lake Tahoe (via Truckee River headwaters).[Pyramid Lake sites](https://pyramidlake.us), [Travel Nevada](https://travelnevada.com)

attractions.name: Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe Museum and Visitors Center
attractions.description: The Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe Museum and Visitors Center is a cultural museum located in Nixon, Nevada, dedicated to preserving and sharing the history, culture, and traditions of the Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe. Exhibits describe the tribe's history and culture, offer insight into why the Paiute people hold the lake and surrounding landscape sacred, and explore Pyramid Lake's natural history and wildlife. The distinctive conical-shaped building was designed by Native American architect Dennis Numkena to resemble a tepee.
attractions.hours: Winter hours (October–April): Monday–Friday, 10:00 a.m.–4:30 p.m. | Summer hours (May–September): Monday–Saturday, 10:00 a.m.–4:30 p.m.
attractions.days_open: Monday–Friday (year-round); Saturday (May–September only); Closed Sundays year-round
attractions.admission: Free (donations welcome)
highlights: Displays on Pyramid Lake Paiute Tribe history and culture
Natural history exhibits about Pyramid Lake and its wildlife
Distinctive tepee-shaped building designed by Native American architect Dennis Numkena
Gift shop featuring handmade Native American crafts and items
Video presentations on tribal heritage (5–25 minute videos)
Information on Pyramid Lake recreation policies and permits
attractions.tips: Admission is free but donations are welcome and help support the museum. The museum is also a visitor center where you can purchase camping, boating, and fishing permits for Pyramid Lake. Consider driving the western shore of Pyramid Lake after your visit for scenic views. The museum is closed on Sundays and Mondays (outside summer Saturday hours), so plan accordingly. Some exhibits include stairs, which may be challenging for visitors with mobility issues.
attractions.nearby: Pyramid Lake (largest remnant of ancient Lake Lahontan), Numana Paiute Tribal Fish Hatchery, Sutcliffe, Pyramid Rock, Truckee River

attractions.name: Berlin-Ichthyosaur State Park
attractions.description: Berlin-Ichthyosaur State Park preserves a turn-of-the-20th-century Nevada ghost town and the world's most abundant concentration of ichthyosaur fossils. The park protects the historic mining town of Berlin, built in the 1890s, along with the Diana Mine. It also houses North America's largest-known remains of ichthyosaurs, ancient marine reptiles that swam in a warm ocean covering central Nevada 225 million years ago, displayed in the park's Fossil House.
attractions.hours: Always Open (365 days a year). Fossil House Tours: April 18 to Memorial Day — 10am and 2pm on Saturdays and Sundays; Memorial Day to Labor Day — 10am and 2pm daily; Labor Day to November 1st — 10am and 2pm on Saturdays and Sundays.
attractions.days_open: Open 7 days a week, 365 days a year
attractions.admission: Day Use: $5.00 per vehicle (Non-NV Vehicles $10.00 per vehicle); Camping: $15.00 per vehicle per night (Non-NV Vehicles $20.00 per night); Bike In: $2.00 per bike; Fossil House Tours: $5 adults, Kids 12 and under free. Cash or check only — no credit cards accepted.
highlights: World's most abundant concentration of ichthyosaur fossils displayed in the Fossil House
Preserved 1890s Nevada ghost town with original buildings and cemetery
Self-guided Berlin Townsite Tour with extensive historical markers
14 campsites open year-round, some suitable for RVs up to 25 feet
Part of the 6,800+ mile American Discovery Trail
Historic Diana Mine site and surrounding desert landscape
attractions.tips: Bring cash or check — the park cannot accept credit cards. No services are available on-site; the nearest gas, food, and phone services are in Gabbs. The town of Ione nearby has no services. Drive only on established roads. Pets must be kept on a 6-foot leash. Drones are prohibited. Check road conditions at nvroads.com before visiting, as the park is in a remote desert location.
attractions.nearby: Ward Charcoal Ovens State Park (Ely, NV), Fort Churchill State Historic Park (Silver Springs, NV), Lahontan State Recreation Area (Silver Springs, NV), Austin (NV ghost town and historic mining site), Ione (NV), Gabbs (NV)

attractions.name: Black Rock City (Burning Man)
attractions.description: Black Rock City is a temporary city built annually in the Black Rock Desert of Nevada, hosting the Burning Man festival — a week-long celebration of art, community, self-expression, and self-reliance. The event features massive art installations, theme camps, mutant vehicles, and culminates in the burning of a giant effigy. It operates under 10 principles including radical inclusion, gifting, and leaving no trace.
attractions.hours: Open continuously during the event (August 30 – September 7, 2026). The city operates 24 hours a day throughout the festival.
attractions.days_open: August 30 – September 7, 2026 (during the annual event only; the city does not exist outside festival dates)
attractions.admission: Sunrise Sale (closed): Give the Gift $975/$1,500/$3,000; Pay Your Way $775; Get the Gift $675. Main Sale (April 29): Get the Gift $550/$675; Pay Your Way $775; Give the Gift $975/$1,500. Ticket Aid Program: $250. Vehicle Pass: $165. All prices plus taxes and fees.
attractions.phone: Not publicly listed; see [Burning Man Help Center](https://help.burningman.org/hc/en-us)
highlights: The Burn — weekly burning of a 50+ foot man effigy and Temple
Massive interactive art installations and sculptures across the playa
Theme camps offering food, activities, performances, and community
Mutant vehicles — art cars that transport participants across the desert
Radical self-expression through costume, art, and participation
Leave No Trace principle — the entire city is dismantled with nothing left behind
attractions.tips: Register for ticket sales in advance as they sell out quickly, especially at lower price tiers. Bring all food, water, shelter, and supplies — nothing is sold in Black Rock City. Prepare for extreme desert conditions: scorching daytime heat, cold nights, and dust storms. Follow Leave No Trace principles strictly. A vehicle pass is required if driving. Consider the Ticket Aid program if on limited income.
attractions.nearby: Gerlach (nearest town, ~30 miles), Black Rock Desert, Pyramid Lake (south), Reno (~100 miles), Winnemucca (~100 miles)

attractions.name: Black Rock Desert Playa
attractions.description: The Black Rock Desert Playa is a vast, flat dry lakebed spanning approximately 200 square miles in northwestern Nevada, a remnant of the ancient Lake Lahontan. It is part of the Black Rock Desert–High Rock Canyon Emigrant Trails National Conservation Area managed by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM), encompassing nearly 120 miles of historic emigrant trails and 10 designated wilderness areas. The playa is famous for hosting the annual Burning Man festival and for land speed record attempts. It offers a unique combination of desert playa, narrow canyons, and mountainous terrain in one of the most remote regions of the Silver State.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily; BLM Black Rock Station Visitor Center: Thursday–Monday 9:00 AM–5:00 PM (April–October, closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays)
attractions.days_open: Daily (24 hours); BLM Visitor Center open Thursday–Monday (April–October)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 200-square-mile dry lakebed (playa) — remnant of ancient Lake Lahontan
Home to the annual Burning Man festival and Black Rock City pop-up metropolis
120 miles of historic emigrant trails including the California and Oregon trails
10 designated wilderness areas within the 800,000-acre Conservation Area
Natural hot springs including Soldier Meadows and Black Rock Hot Springs
Land speed record venue (763 mph set in 1997)
attractions.tips: The area is extremely remote with no cell phone signal — bring extra water, food, and clothing. High-clearance vehicles with off-road tires (at least 6-ply) and spare tires are strongly recommended. Do not drive on the playa unless it is completely dry or your vehicle will get stuck in the mud. Avoid abandoned mines and hot springs — the BLM does not manage these for recreational use and people have been severely injured or killed. Fires are not allowed directly on the playa floor. Best times to visit are spring and fall; summer temperatures can exceed 100°F. Stop by the Friends of Black Rock-High Rock Visitor Center at 320 Main Street in Gerlach for free maps and current playa conditions.
attractions.nearby: Pyramid Lake, Fly Geyser (viewable via guided tours), Gerlach, Empire, Soldier Meadows Hot Springs, High Rock Canyon, Steamboat Rock Trail, Planet X Pottery (near Gerlach)

attractions.name: Bowers Mansion Regional Park
attractions.description: Bowers Mansion Regional Park is a 52-acre regional park in Washoe Valley featuring the historic Bowers Mansion, a beautifully restored 1860s Victorian-era home built by Comstock Lode millionaires Sandy and Eilley Bowers. The park offers guided mansion tours, a seasonal heated outdoor pool fed by natural hot springs, picnic areas, playgrounds, and hiking trails to the Bowers family cemetery with scenic valley views.
attractions.address: 4005 Bowers Mansion Road, New Washoe City, NV 89704
attractions.hours: Park grounds: Spring 8:00 AM–7:00 PM, Summer 8:00 AM–9:00 PM, Fall 8:00 AM–7:00 PM, Winter 8:00 AM–5:00 PM. Mansion Tours: 11:00 AM, 12:00 PM, 1:00 PM, 2:00 PM, 3:00 PM (seasonal). Pool: 12:00 PM–5:00 PM daily (extended to 7:00 PM Tue/Thu/Fri in summer).
attractions.days_open: Park grounds open daily. Mansion tours on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays (Memorial Day through October 31; Fridays June 19–August 7).
attractions.admission: Park grounds: Free. Mansion Tours: Adults (18–61) $10, Seniors (62+) $7, Children (6–17) $7, Children 5 and under free. Pool: $5 per person (special rates for kids/infants/seniors).
highlights: Historic 1860s Victorian mansion built by Comstock Lode millionaires Sandy and Eilley Bowers
Guided mansion tours with period furnishings showcasing Nevada's Silver Rush era
Seasonal heated outdoor pool fed by natural hot springs
Scenic hiking trails leading to the Bowers family cemetery with Washoe Valley views
Picnic pavilions and individual picnic sites with BBQ grills
Two children's playgrounds and expansive grassy areas
attractions.tips: Mansion tours are seasonal (late May through late October) and only available on weekends and holidays — verify the schedule before visiting. Dogs are not allowed except for service animals. Bring cash for mansion tour admission. Visit on weekdays to enjoy the park grounds without crowds. Bring a blanket for Programs on the Porch evening events. The hike to the cemetery is moderately strenuous with benches along the way.
attractions.nearby: Davis Creek Regional Park (campground and hiking trails), Washoe Lake State Recreation Area, Carson City (state capital ~15 min south), Reno (~20 min north), Galena Creek Regional Park

attractions.name: Churchill County Museum and Archives
attractions.description: The Churchill County Museum and Archives is a public-private partnership located on historic Route 50 in Fallon, Nevada. Known as the "Best Little Museum on the Loneliest Road in America," it preserves and showcases the local history and culture of Churchill County. The museum offers exhibits on the area's heritage, archives, and manages the nearby Hidden Cave archaeological site.
attractions.hours: Summer (March–November): Tuesday–Saturday 10am–5pm, Sunday 10am–3pm. Winter (December–February): Tuesday–Saturday 10am–4pm, Sunday 10am–3pm. Closed Mondays year-round.
attractions.admission: Free (suggested donation of $5; waived for families on food assistance as part of Museums for All)
highlights: Named 'Best Little Museum on the Loneliest Road in America' on historic Route 50
Manages Hidden Cave Archaeological Site tours (2nd and 4th Saturdays)
Extensive local history exhibits and archives
Woodliff Store exhibit
Free admission with suggested donation
Wheelchair accessible (all exhibits except Woodliff Store)
attractions.tips: Plan for a 90-minute suggested visit time. Free parking is available in the museum lot and on the street. Hidden Cave tours require arriving by 9:15am as orientation begins at 9:30am sharp. The museum is closed on Mondays, so plan accordingly.
attractions.nearby: Hidden Cave Archaeological Site, Grimes Point Archaeological Site, Naval Air Station Fallon, Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge, Fallon Convention Center

attractions.name: Goldfield Hotel
attractions.description: The Goldfield Hotel is a historic four-story building in Goldfield, Nevada, designed in the Classical Revival style and built between 1907-1908. Once the most luxurious hotel between Denver and San Francisco with 154 rooms, telephones, and private baths, it is now a contributing property of the Goldfield Historic District on the National Register of Historic Places. The hotel is famous for its reputation as one of the most haunted buildings in America and has been featured on paranormal investigation TV shows. It is currently closed to the public for general tours but offers limited guided tours and paranormal investigations by appointment.
attractions.hours: Tours by appointment only. Typically: Thursday nights at 6:00 PM, Fridays 12:00 PM to 9:00 PM, Saturdays 6:00 PM to 9:00 PM. Closed Sunday through Wednesday.
attractions.admission: Guided tours: $20 per person. Paranormal investigations: priced based on size and scope. Contact Heather Ingalls at (775) 277-0484 to book.
highlights: Built in 1907-1908 as the most luxurious hotel between Denver and San Francisco
154 rooms each with private baths, telephones, and electric lighting — rare luxuries for the era
Featured on Ghost Adventures and other paranormal investigation shows as one of America's most haunted buildings
Contributing property to the Goldfield Historic District, listed on the National Register of Historic Places
Classical Revival architecture designed by prominent Reno architects Holesworth and Curtis
Building constructed over the main headframe of an abandoned gold mine
attractions.tips: Tours are by appointment only — contact Heather Ingalls at (775) 277-0484. The hotel has limited lighting, so bring your own flashlight. There is no heating or air conditioning, no working elevator (five flights of stairs), and no on-site restrooms. Wear comfortable walking shoes and dress appropriately for the weather. The hotel is also part of a National Historic District — consider donating to or becoming a member of the Goldfield Historical Society.
attractions.nearby: International Car Forest of the Last Church, Goldfield Cemetery, Esmeralda County Courthouse, Goldfield High School (historic), Goldfield Ghost Town attractions

attractions.name: Graeagle Feather River Area
attractions.description: Graeagle is a charming mountain community in the High Sierras of Plumas County, California, centered around the Wild and Scenic Middle Fork Feather River. Founded as a lumber mill town in 1916, it has evolved into a renowned golf destination with six championship courses, surrounded by dense forests, alpine lakes, and outdoor recreation. The area offers year-round activities including hiking, fishing, golfing, snowshoeing, and cultural events like the Cowboy Poetry & Music Show and the annual Graeagle Arts & Crafts Fair.
attractions.hours: Varies by specific attraction and season. The Graeagle area is open year-round. Individual businesses, golf courses, and parks have their own hours. Golf courses typically open in late spring (April/May).
attractions.days_open: Year-round (varies by specific attraction and season)
attractions.admission: Free to visit the town area; individual attractions vary (golf courses, ski bowl, museums have separate fees)
highlights: Six championship golf courses including The Dragon at Nakoma Resort and Whitehawk Ranch
Wild and Scenic Middle Fork Feather River for fishing, kayaking, and rafting
Lakes Basin Recreation Area with 35+ alpine lakes and 100+ miles of hiking trails
Graeagle Mill Pond with paddle boats, swimming, swimming beach, and summer concerts
Plumas-Eureka State Park with gold mining history, Johnsville Museum, and Madora Lake Loop
Annual events including Cowboy Poetry & Music Show, Arts & Crafts Fair, and Independence Day fireworks
attractions.tips: Graeagle is about one hour west of Reno, Nevada and 2.5 hours northeast of Sacramento. Summer is peak season; winter offers snowshoeing and cross-country skiing. Book golf tee times in advance as courses are highly rated. The area is at high elevation (around 4,000-7,000 feet), so bring layers and stay hydrated. Many shops and restaurants are concentrated along Highway 89 in downtown Graeagle. Check conditions of recreation facilities before visiting as some areas may still be recovering from the 2021 Dixie Fire.
attractions.nearby: Nakoma Resort (Clio), Lake Davis Recreation Area, Portola Western Pacific Railroad Museum, Plumas County Museum (Quincy), Bucks Lake, Gold Lake Highway, Feather River Canyon Scenic Byway, The Brewing Lair (Blairsden), Sardine Lake Lodge

attractions.name: Great Basin National Park
attractions.description: Great Basin National Park is a remote national park in eastern Nevada, centered around the 13,063-foot Wheeler Peak and the ancient Lehman Caves. The park protects the fragile ecosystem of the Great Basin desert, featuring bristlecone pine groves, alpine lakes, and some of the darkest night skies in the continental United States. It is known for its exceptional stargazing opportunities, including the annual Astronomy Festival.
attractions.hours: Park grounds open 24 hours daily. Lehman Caves Visitor Center: typically 8:00 AM - 4:00 PM (seasonal, currently closed for renovations through summer 2026). Wheeler Peak Scenic Drive: closed at Mather Overlook for winter (opens seasonally).
attractions.admission: Park entrance: Free (no entrance pass required). Lehman Caves tours: per-person fee varies by tour (Gothic Palace, Parachute Shield, Lodge Room, Grand Palace, Introduction to Wild Caving, First Room Accessible). Camping: $20 per night per campsite. RV dump station: $10. America the Beautiful Senior Pass holders receive 50% discount on camping.
highlights: Wheeler Peak - 13,063 ft summit, Nevada's second-highest peak
Lehman Caves - ancient limestone caverns with rare shield formations
Ancient Bristlecone Pine groves - some trees over 4,000 years old
Dark night skies - International Dark Sky Park with exceptional stargazing
Alpine lakes including Stella and Teresa Lakes
Wheeler Peak Scenic Drive - 12-mile paved road to 11,000+ ft elevation
attractions.tips: Lehman Caves and Visitor Center are closed through summer 2026 for electrical renovations - check before visiting. Wheeler Peak Scenic Drive is currently closed at Mather Overlook for winter. Reservations are strongly recommended for cave tours via Recreation.gov. The park is remote - fill up on gas in Baker or Delta, UT before arriving. Bring layers as temperatures vary dramatically with elevation. Visit during new moon for best stargazing.
attractions.nearby: Ely (NV), Delta (UT), Snake Range, Lehman Caves Visitor Center, Baker town, Little Sahara Recreation Area (UT)

attractions.name: International Car Forest of the Last Church
attractions.description: The International Car Forest of the Last Church is an open-air art installation located just outside Goldfield, Nevada, featuring more than 40 rusted vehicles partially buried upright in the desert. Created by artist Mark Rippie and collaborator Chad Sorg, the vehicles are covered in ever-changing graffiti art, making it one of the largest free open-air art galleries in Nevada. The site is accessible 24/7 along the Free-Range Art Highway and offers a unique, surreal desert experience.
attractions.hours: Open 24/7 (best enjoyed from sunrise to sunset)
attractions.admission: Free (donations appreciated)
highlights: Over 40 vehicles partially buried upright in the desert
Ever-changing graffiti and spray-paint art covering each vehicle
Created by artist Mark Rippie and collaborator Chad Sorg
Open 24/7 with no admission fee
Accessible via dirt road just east of US Highway 95
Port-o-potty restroom available at entrance
attractions.tips: The dirt road is passable for regular cars — just drive slowly. Best visited at sunset for dramatic lighting and photography. Watch out for rattlesnakes and sharp rusty metal. Donations are appreciated to support the site. Don't add your own graffiti — respect the existing art. Cell service is decent at the location.
attractions.nearby: Goldfield Historic Cemetery, Goldfield Hotel, Rocket Bob's Art Cars, Tonopah (about 30 miles north)

attractions.name: Lahontan State Recreation Area
attractions.description: Lahontan State Recreation Area is a public recreation area surrounding Lake Lahontan, a 17-mile-long impoundment of the Carson River located approximately 18 miles west of Fallon, Nevada. The park features 69 miles of shoreline and is a popular destination for boating, fishing, water-skiing, horseback riding, camping, and hiking year-round. It receives approximately 350,000 visitors annually and is managed by the Nevada Division of State Parks.
attractions.hours: Park: Open 7 days a week, 365 days a year (sunrise to sunset for day use). Fee Booth: 8 a.m. - 4 p.m. daily. Silver Springs Ranger Station Office: 10 a.m. - 12:30 p.m., Friday - Tuesday (closed Wednesdays and Thursdays).
attractions.days_open: Seven days a week, 365 days a year
attractions.admission: Day use entrance fee: $5 per vehicle (Nevada residents), $10 per vehicle (non-NV). Boat launch: $15 (Nevada residents), $20 (non-NV). Camping: $15 per night (Nevada residents), $20 per night (non-NV). Camp and boat: $25 per night (Nevada residents), $30 per night (non-NV). Bike in: $2 per bike.
highlights: 69 miles of shoreline around Lahontan Reservoir
Multiple sandy beaches (Beaches 22-31) for swimming and sunbathing
Boat launching and water sports including water-skiing and fishing
Year-round camping with developed and primitive sites
Silver Springs Beach #7 with restrooms, tables, and grills
Equestrian trails and horseback riding opportunities
attractions.tips: Showers and flush toilets are closed during the off-season from October to May. Day use hours are sunrise to sunset. Speed limit on beaches is 15 MPH. Quiet hours are 10 PM to 7 AM. Campsite check-in starts at 2 PM and check-out is by 11 AM. Metal detecting is permitted from October 1 to April 1. The entrance station can have long lines during peak season — arrive early. Off-season entry is free. Bring plenty of water and sun protection as there is limited shade.
attractions.nearby: Fort Churchill State Historic Park, Lahontan Dam, Silver Springs community, Grimes Point Archaeological Area, Fallon Naval Air Station Museum, Shoshone Indian Casino

attractions.name: Lassen Volcanic National Park
attractions.description: Lassen Volcanic National Park in northern California is home to steaming fumaroles, meadows freckled with wildflowers, clear mountain lakes, and numerous volcanoes. Lassen Peak, the largest plug dome volcano in the world, towers above the park and last erupted in 1915. The park showcases all four types of volcanoes found on Earth: plug dome, shield, cinder cone, and composite.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily, year-round. The main park road (Hwy 89) is seasonally closed December through late spring/early summer due to snow. Visitor centers have seasonal operating hours.
attractions.admission: Standard Entrance Pass: Private Vehicle $30 (Apr 16–Nov 30) / $10 (Dec 1–Apr 15); Motorcycle $25 (Apr 16–Nov 30) / $10 (Dec 1–Apr 15); Per Person (bicycle/foot) $15 (Apr 16–Nov 30) / $10 (Dec 1–Apr 15). Annual Park Pass: $55. America the Beautiful Pass accepted. Cashless park—credit/debit only. Education/Academic Groups: Free.
highlights: Lassen Peak - the largest plug dome volcano in the world, last erupted 1915
Bumpass Hell - largest hydrothermal area in the park with boiling mud pots and fumaroles
Four types of volcanoes in one park: plug dome, shield, cinder cone, and composite
Scenic parkway with over 150 miles of hiking trails
Crystal-clear alpine lakes including Manzanita Lake and Emerald Lake
Cinder Cone and Fantastic Lava Beds volcanic landscape
attractions.tips: The main park road (Hwy 89) is seasonally closed due to snow, typically December through late spring. Check road conditions before visiting. The park is cashless—bring a credit or debit card. Free entrance days are available on select federal holidays. Visit in summer or early fall for the best road and trail access. Download the NPS app for offline maps and information.
attractions.nearby: Lassen National Forest, Shasta-Trinity National Forest, McArthur-Burney Falls Memorial State Park, Manzanita Lake, Whiskeytown National Recreation Area, Lava Beds National Monument

attractions.name: Mustang Monument Wild Horse Eco Resort
attractions.description: Mustang Monument is a luxury wild horse eco-resort and preserve located near Wells, Nevada, offering an all-inclusive "American Safari" experience. Guests stay in hand-painted luxury tipis or western safari cottages while surrounded by hundreds of wild mustangs roaming free on the property. The resort combines 5-star accommodations and gourmet dining with authentic western activities, all while supporting American wild horse conservation.
attractions.hours: Open year-round; seasonal operation (typically June through September for full operations, check website for current schedule)
attractions.admission: Rates approximately $2,400-$2,800 USD per room per night (all-inclusive, includes lodging, meals, soft drinks, beer/wine, and most activities). Children under 12: $400/night. Rates do not include taxes and service charges. Two-day packages available if availability permits.
highlights: Luxury hand-painted tipis and western safari cottages with 5-star amenities
Home to hundreds of free-roaming wild mustangs on a large preserve
All-inclusive rates covering gourmet meals, beverages, accommodations, and activities
Michelin-star chef cuisine and curated wine selections
Authentic western activities including horseback riding and mustang viewing
Supports American wild horse conservation and adoption programs
attractions.tips: Book well in advance as the resort has only 20 rooms (10 tipis and 10 cottages) and limited availability. The resort operates primarily June through September, so plan accordingly. Minimum stays may apply. The property is remote — located on South Highway 93 near Wells, about 2.5 hours from Elko. Cell service may be limited on the property.
attractions.nearby: Ruby Mountains, Elko (approx. 55 miles), Interstate 80 corridor, Angel Lake, Lamoille Canyon, and the historic railroad town of Wells

attractions.name: Naval Air Station Fallon Air Park (NAS Fallon Heritage Museum)
attractions.description: The Naval Air Station Fallon Air Park is a military aviation museum located on NAS Fallon, home of the Navy's TOPGUN school. It features an outdoor collection of preserved U.S. Navy and opposing force aircraft from the Korean War through Desert Storm eras. The museum is situated on an active military base and requires base access for visitors, making it a unique but restricted attraction for aviation enthusiasts.
attractions.hours: Limited access; open-air museum on active military base. Base gate hours typically 0600-2200 for authorized personnel. Museum area is accessible when on base.
attractions.admission: Free (military base access required)
highlights: Outdoor air park with preserved U.S. Navy aircraft from Korean War to Desert Storm eras
Home to the Naval Aviation Warfighting Development Center (TOPGUN)
Collection includes opposing force aircraft such as Soviet-era MiG fighters
Features aircraft like F-14 Tomcat, F-4 Phantom, A-6 Intruder, F/A-18 Hornet, and F-16 Fighting Falcon
Free admission for those with base access
Self-guided tour of static aircraft displays with informational plaques
attractions.tips: Access requires military base clearance — civilians need an escort from military personnel or prior authorization. Contact the base visitor center at 775-426-8257 before visiting. The museum is an outdoor air park, so dress appropriately for desert weather. Photography is generally permitted in the museum area. Local events and open house days may offer civilian access — check with the base public affairs office.
attractions.nearby: Churchill County Museum & Archives (1050 S Maine St, Fallon), Hidden Cave archaeological site, Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge, Fallon Paiute-Shoshone Tribe Cultural Center, Grimes Point Archaeological Area

attractions.name: Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge
attractions.description: Stillwater National Wildlife Refuge is an 80,000+ acre oasis for migratory waterfowl, shorebirds, wildlife, and plants located in the Lahontan Valley of north-central Nevada. The refuge attracts more than a quarter million waterfowl, hundreds of thousands of shorebirds, and over 20,000 other water birds, with more than 290 species of birds sighted in the area. It offers diverse wetland habitats, hiking trails, auto tour routes, and hunting opportunities in a remote desert setting.
attractions.hours: Office Hours: Monday – Friday, 8:00 AM – 4:30 PM. Closed on all federal holidays or when all staff are away to work on field projects. The refuge grounds themselves are open sunrise to sunset.
attractions.days_open: Monday through Friday (office); refuge grounds open daily sunrise to sunset
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: 80,000+ acres of diverse wetland and desert landscapes
Over 290 species of birds sighted in the area
Auto Tour Loop around Lower Stillwater Point Reservoir
Five designated hiking trails including the Tule Trail and ADA-compliant observation platforms
Hunting opportunities for waterfowl, upland game, and big game on designated areas
Remote 'Oasis in the Desert' setting with minimal development
attractions.tips: The refuge is remote and undeveloped with unreliable cell phone service — bring plenty of water, food, fuel, and emergency supplies. Check weather, roads, and recent wildlife sightings before visiting. Restrooms are available at Stillwater Point Reservoir and the pavilion on the auto tour route. Bring binoculars for birding. The refuge is part of the Stillwater NWR Complex which also includes Anaho Island NWR and Fallon NWR.
attractions.nearby: Fallon National Wildlife Refuge, Anaho Island National Wildlife Refuge, Churchill County Museum, Fallon Convention Center, Grimes Point Archaeological Area, Dixie Valley

attractions.name: Walker Lake Recreation Area
attractions.description: Walker Lake is a large natural lake in west-central Nevada and the last remnant of ancient Lake Lahontan, which once covered much of northwestern Nevada. Now managed by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM), the Walker Lake Recreation Area offers swimming, boating, camping, bird watching, and water sports along the lake's shoreline. The lake sits at the base of Mt. Grant, one of Nevada's tallest peaks, and serves as a prime stopover for migratory birds on the Pacific Flyway. Although declining water levels have impacted native fish populations, the area remains a popular destination for outdoor recreation with multiple BLM campgrounds along its western shore.
attractions.address: Along U.S. Route 95, north of Hawthorne, NV
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours a day, 7 days a week (subject to seasonal weather-related closures)
attractions.admission: No day-use entrance fee. Developed campsite: $6/night/vehicle, $36/week/vehicle. Primitive campsite: $2/night/vehicle, $22/week/vehicle. America the Beautiful Senior and Access Passes honored.
highlights: 30,000-acre natural lake, remnant of ancient Lake Lahontan
Spectacular views of 11,245-foot Mt. Grant
Multiple BLM campgrounds: Sportsman's Beach, Tamarack Beach, and 20 Mile Beach
Prime bird watching with over 10,000 water birds including pelicans, loons, egrets, and herons
Swimming, boating, kayaking, windsurfing, and water-skiing opportunities
Desert Bighorn Sheep and wild mustang sightings along the shoreline
attractions.tips: No potable water is available at Sportsman's Beach — bring all water you need. The lake's high salinity prevents fishing; fish at nearby Walker River State Recreation Area instead. Boat ramps are primitive and may require a 4-wheel drive vehicle due to receding shoreline. The area is extremely remote — gas up in Hawthorne before visiting. Best visited spring through fall; winter can have weather-related closures. Pack out all trash as this is primitive camping territory.
attractions.nearby: Hawthorne Ordnance Museum, Walker River State Recreation Area, Mono Lake (California), Bodie State Historic Park, Boundary Peak (Nevada's highest mountain), Travertine Hot Springs, Yerington
